blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
This session is the first major synthesis point in the workshop — the moment when all three prior streams of self-discovery (purpose, DISC, LSA) flow together into a single, integrated portrait of each participant's leadership profile. The instrument for this integration is the Leadership Tree, the DVULI's signature summary tool. The tree metaphor is theologically rich and pedagogically efficient: the Roots (the participant's personal purpose statement) supply the invisible nourishment that makes everything else possible; the Trunk (the people who support the leader) provides the structural support without which no growth is sustainable; and the Branches and Leaves (DISC style and LSA strategies) are the visible, outward expressions of leadership that others observe and experience.

The central claim of the session is that a healthy tree can live for a very long time, but one with damaged or underdeveloped roots, trunk, or branches soon dies. This is not a metaphor for ambitious ministry programs — it is a metaphor for the leader themselves. Many urban ministry leaders have impressive branches (visible activity) with compromised roots (unclear or forgotten purpose) and insufficient trunk (absent or inadequate support networks). This session invites participants to be honest about which parts of their tree are thriving and which are struggling. The DVULI's pre-assignment data — purpose statement, DISC natural style, LSA preferred strategies, and mentor/support community — is collated and displayed on the Leadership Tree poster, creating an artifact each participant will carry forward into every subsequent session.

The first integration the session develops is the relationship between purpose and DISC profile. A participant's purpose statement answers the question of why they lead. Their DISC profile describes how that purpose will most naturally move through them into action. A High-D leader whose purpose centers on justice advocacy will likely pursue that purpose through direct confrontation of systems, measurable outcomes, and fast decisions — which means their purpose is real, but their blind spot is the relational damage that speed and directness can leave behind. A High-S leader with the same justice purpose will pursue it through patient coalition-building, consistent presence, and deep loyalty to the people affected — which means their purpose is also real, but their challenge is that systemic change often requires the kind of confrontation that feels threatening to their preferred style. The point is not that one approach is better. The point is that DISC does not create purpose, it routes it — and leaders who understand that routing can make deliberate choices about when to lead from their natural style and when to adapt. Participants who map their own purpose statement against their DISC profile in this session frequently discover either that their stated purpose and their behavioral style are well-aligned, or that there is friction between what they say they are about and how they actually tend to operate under pressure. Both discoveries are valuable.

The second integration connects LSA learning strategies to the question of which leadership behaviors will come naturally and which will require deliberate effort. The Learning Style Analysis reveals not just how a person prefers to receive information, but how they tend to process new challenges, structure their thinking, and respond when the environment changes unexpectedly. A participant whose LSA shows strong analytic and sequential preferences will likely find strategic planning, systems design, and structured mentoring relationships to be genuine strengths — those activities match how their brain is already wired to work. The same person may find that improvised pastoral conversations, creative brainstorming sessions, or highly relational team dynamics require conscious energy rather than flowing naturally. This does not mean those behaviors are off-limits. It means they are developmental edges, not defaults. When participants locate their LSA profile on the Leadership Tree alongside their DISC style, a pattern becomes visible: some branches grow easily, some require deliberate cultivation, and that differentiation is data, not deficit.

To make this concrete, consider a composite example drawn from DVULI participant profiles. A leader whose purpose statement centers on "restoring dignity to marginalized youth" arrives with a High-I DISC profile — relational, expressive, optimistic — and an LSA that shows strong interpersonal and kinesthetic learning preferences. Her natural mode is to enter a room, read people quickly, build trust through story and energy, and mobilize others through enthusiasm. Her Leadership Tree, honestly assessed, shows strong branches in the relational quadrant and in community activation. Her roots — the purpose statement — are clear and genuinely motivating. But her trunk reveals a gap: she has relied almost entirely on informal peer relationships for support, has no formal mentor, and has not built the reflective practices that would help her learn from her own experience over time. Her tree is not unhealthy — but it is asymmetrical. The session's work is not to celebrate what is strong; it is to name what is uneven and begin the process of correction before the asymmetry becomes structural damage.

That corrective process is what the second half of the session addresses through two disciplines: mentoring and reflective journaling. Drawing on Fred Smith's principles from Mentoring That Matters — including the eight characteristics of effective mentors and the responsibilities of the mentee — participants begin identifying mentor candidates who can speak specifically into the developmental edges their integrated profile has surfaced. A High-C leader with sequential LSA preferences needs a different kind of mentor conversation than the High-I leader described above, and naming that difference is part of the session's work. Douglas Rumford's principles for personal journaling, including the spiraling practice of returning to earlier entries with fresh insight, introduce the reflective loop methodology participants will use throughout the program and that will culminate in their Breakthrough Plan's Personal Learning Journal. Journaling, in this framework, is not therapy and it is not productivity tracking — it is the practice by which leaders begin to notice the distance between their roots and their branches, and close it intentionally over time.

By the end of this session, participants hold a Leadership Tree poster that is not a summary of three separate assessments — it is a single integrated portrait. Purpose explains the why. DISC explains the how. LSA explains the where growth is natural and where it costs something. Mentoring and journaling are the ongoing practices that keep the whole tree alive. Participants who leave this session with all four layers clearly named and honestly assessed have what they need to engage every subsequent chapter as a unified self rather than a collection of scores.

Session 4

The Leadership Tree: Roots, Trunk, Branches, and the Harvest of Your Design

Integrating purpose, support, and behavioral style into a single portrait — and building the disciplines that will sustain growth for a lifetime

Consider Marcus, a youth director who has been running three weekly programs, a mentoring cohort, a summer camp, and a community mural project simultaneously for six years. When people in the city think of urban youth ministry, they think of Marcus. His calendar is full. His Instagram is full. His building is full. But Marcus hasn't revisited his personal purpose statement since he wrote it in a seminary class nearly a decade ago. He hasn't met regularly with a mentor since the one person who filled that role moved to another state two years ago. And when his DISC report landed in his inbox last week, he found himself staring at the word "Adapted Style" for a long time, realizing he has been performing a leadership role that was designed to impress others rather than express who he actually is.

Marcus's branches are spectacular. His roots have been quietly dying. Sessions 1 through 3 of this workshop have equipped you with three distinct and powerful lenses for self-understanding: a personal purpose statement rooted in the three-fold mission, a DISC Natural Style profile revealing your behavioral wiring, and an LSA strategy report mapping how you actually deploy leadership toward those you serve. Session 4 is the first major synthesis point of the program — the moment when those three streams of data converge into a single, integrated portrait called the Leadership Tree. This session is not an ending; it is an orientation. The tree you build today will be the map you carry into every subsequent session, every group dialogue, and ultimately into your Breakthrough Plan.

The Architecture of a Living Tree

The Leadership Tree is the DVULI program's signature synthesis tool. Its power lies not in its complexity but in its completeness. Unlike analytical frameworks that isolate a single dimension of leadership — personality alone, or strategy alone, or purpose alone — the tree metaphor insists that all three must coexist and interoperate if a leader is to thrive. Gosling and Mintzberg (2003) argued forcefully that effective leadership development requires integrative metaphors: models that hold multiple dimensions simultaneously, allowing learners to perceive relationships and tensions rather than isolated competencies. The tree is precisely such a model.

The DVULI workbook frames the three structural elements with clarity: "Leaves and Branches are the parts of your leadership that are visible to others, just like the leaves and branches on a tree. This includes your Behavioral Style (Biblical DISC) and your Leadership Strategy (LSA). The Trunk represents the people who support you, just like how the trunk of the tree supports the branches. The Roots are just as extensive and important as the leaves and branches but cannot be outwardly seen by others. They include a clearly defined purpose" (LW1 Pre-Assignment, 2024, p. 13).

What makes this architecture theologically as well as pedagogically compelling is the insistence that a healthy tree can live for a very long time, but one with damaged or underdeveloped parts soon dies. This is not primarily a metaphor for ministry programs. It is a metaphor for the leader themselves. Research by Harbaugh and Furst (1994) found that urban ministry leaders face significantly elevated burnout risk compared to other pastoral contexts, and that the three primary predictors are role ambiguity (unclear purpose), social isolation (inadequate support networks), and unprocessed stress (absence of reflective practices) — a finding that maps with striking precision onto the three structural elements of the Leadership Tree. When Marcus's roots begin to die, it is only a matter of time before the entire tree follows, no matter how impressive the canopy appears to onlookers.

The Roots: Purpose as the Invisible Foundation

In a living tree, roots are the last part of the structure anyone sees. They extend deep into the ground, far wider than the visible canopy above, working in silence to draw up everything the tree needs to survive and bear fruit. Remove the roots and the tree — no matter how magnificent it appears — will be dead within a season. The same is true of leadership. Craig and Snook (2014) found in their research with Harvard Business School executives that fewer than 20% of leaders carry a clear, actionable personal purpose, and that this gap between purpose and action is the primary factor in leadership derailment. The leader who has not settled the question of "why am I here?" will eventually find themselves unable to answer "where am I going?" with any conviction.

In Session 1, you worked through the three-fold mission from Richard Bolles's What Color Is Your Parachute? and drafted a personal purpose statement. Recall the three layers: Purpose Part One — to know God and enjoy Him forever; Purpose Part Two — to make the world a better place moment by moment; and Purpose Part Three — to exercise the particular talent you came to Earth to use, in the setting God has most prepared you for, toward the purposes He most needs done. Your purpose statement was the attempt to synthesize these three layers into a sentence or two you could carry with you. That statement is what belongs in the Roots section of your Leadership Tree.

There is an important developmental note embedded in the DVULI workbook's treatment of purpose: "Our purpose doesn't change; our understanding and growth in it may. As our roles or settings change, the way we express our purpose may stay the same, shift, or expand" (LW1 Workbook, 2024, p. 7). This means that the roots grow deeper over the course of the program. The purpose statement you bring to Session 4 is a beginning, not a final answer. In the Second National Conference, you will revisit your personal purpose statement in the context of systems thinking. At the conclusion of the program, your Breakthrough Plan will be anchored in the purpose you have been refining all along. Bill George and colleagues (2007) found in their landmark study of 125 diverse leaders that authentic leadership emerges precisely from this kind of ongoing, story-rooted engagement with one's own purpose — not from adopting external frameworks, but from the patient clarification of what one's own life has been saying all along.

Think About It

Before reading on, take two minutes to locate the personal purpose statement you wrote in Session 1. Read it aloud. Does it still feel accurate? Does it feel alive? Does it feel like something you would actually want to build your leadership on — or does it feel more like an academic exercise you completed to move on to the next task? The honesty with which you answer that question is the beginning of healthy roots.

The Trunk: Support as the Hidden Structure

A tree's trunk is its most visible structural feature from the outside, and yet most people see the trunk as merely the conduit between roots and branches — not the focus. In the Leadership Tree, however, the trunk carries specific meaning: it represents the people who support and pray for the leader. The Pre-Assignment poster designates two categories explicitly: people who pray for the leader and people who support and encourage the leader. This is not incidental. The trunk, in arboricultural terms, is the load-bearing column that allows a tree to stand upright against wind and weather and to channel nutrients from the roots to the canopy. A tree with a compromised trunk will eventually collapse under the weight of its own branches, regardless of how healthy the roots and leaves may be.

Kram's (1985) foundational research on mentoring introduced a concept that directly illuminates the trunk: the mentor constellation. Rather than positing a single transformative mentor relationship, Kram's research found that effective development occurs when leaders maintain a network of diverse support relationships, each serving different functions. Some relationships primarily serve career-development functions — sponsorship, exposure, coaching, protection, challenging assignments. Others serve psychosocial functions — role modeling, acceptance, counseling, friendship. Both categories are essential; neither is sufficient alone. The people who belong in the Trunk section of your Leadership Tree are not only formal mentors; they are everyone who holds your development in their prayers and provides the relational scaffolding that lets you grow.

Daloz (1999) offered a complementary framework. He argued that effective developmental relationships involve three core functions: support (providing the emotional safety in which growth becomes possible), challenge (disrupting the comfortable but limiting assumptions that keep a leader from realizing their potential), and vision (holding a clear picture of who the mentee can become before the mentee can see it for themselves). Notice that support alone — without challenge and vision — can create comfort without growth. Trunk-sized relationships, in Daloz's terms, are not simply people who make you feel good about yourself. They are people willing to tell you the truth.

The workbook instruction for the trunk section is deliberately open-ended: "The trunk of the tree includes information you will generate yourself" (LW1 Pre-Assignment, 2024, p. 13). This is because the health of your support network is entirely your own work. No assessment tool can tell you whether you have a thriving community of people who pray for you and speak truth into your life. Only you know whether the names you write in the trunk section represent real, active relationships — or whether that section of your tree is, in Marcus's words, already hollow.

The Branches and Leaves: The Visible Expression of Your Design

While roots and trunk do their quiet, foundational work, branches and leaves are what the world sees. They are the observable, outward expression of the leader's design — the behavioral patterns and strategic preferences that others experience when they encounter your leadership in action. The DVULI Leadership Tree poster designates the following elements for the Branches and Leaves section: your Natural DISC Style Pattern name, your Natural DISC Style Pattern letters, your Chosen Biblical Character associated with your natural style, your Highest Plot Point Letter and its associated traits, your Preferred Leadership Strategies from the LSA, and your Most Frequently Chosen and Least Frequently Chosen strategy.

Together, these elements answer the question that every person you lead is unconsciously asking: How does this leader tend to show up? A leader with a high Dominance natural style will show up with directness and a push-for-results energy. A leader with a high Steadiness orientation will show up with patient, open supportiveness. Neither is superior; both reflect the diverse design of the body of Christ. The DISC workbook captures this beautifully in its opening devotional premise from 1 Peter 4:10–11: every believer has been entrusted with gift(s) as stewardship, not ownership. Our behavioral style is not something we chose; it is something we have been given to manage wisely for the sake of others.

The LSA dimension of the branches adds a critical layer that DISC alone cannot provide. While DISC reveals how you tend to behave, the LSA reveals how you tend to deploy your leadership energy in relation to specific task-and-performer combinations. Your most frequently chosen strategy indicates the default mode to which you naturally return under pressure or when you are not paying close attention. Your least frequently chosen strategy points to the developmental frontier — the mode that may be most needed by members of your team but least available in your natural repertoire. Kolb (1984) observed that learning deepens only when all four stages of the experiential cycle are engaged; similarly, leadership effectiveness deepens only when all four situational strategies are accessible and flexibly deployed. The branches section of your tree maps both your current strengths and your growing edges simultaneously.

Mentoring That Matters: Fred Smith's Eight Characteristics

The second half of Session 4 shifts from mapping your current state to building the disciplines that will sustain growth throughout the entire arc of the program and beyond. The first of these disciplines is mentoring. The DVULI program structures a very specific mentoring relationship — one with a Core Values mentor whose primary role is helping you understand and apply the program's Core Values to your life and ministry. Fred Smith's "Mentoring That Matters," included in the Pre-Assignment, provides the human and relational architecture within which that formal relationship must operate.

Smith opens with a crucial distinction: genuine mentoring is "a one-on-one relationship between a mentor and mentoree for the specific and definable development of a skill or an art." Church programs that pair older men with younger men for general Bible study or prayer are not, in Smith's view, mentoring — they are fellowship, which is valuable but different. Mentoring requires a specific agenda, a directional purpose, and a willingness on both sides to inhabit an asymmetrical learning relationship rather than a peer one. This precision of definition matters enormously in the DVULI context, because the Core Values mentor is not simply a friend or an accountability partner. The mentor is being asked to hold the mentee accountable to growth in specific, articulated dimensions of leadership character.

Smith identifies eight characteristics that must be present in some combination for mentoring to be effective. It is worth reading each with care, because they reveal as much about the ideal mentee posture as they do about the ideal mentor:

  1. Compatible philosophy. Mentor and mentee must share foundational commitments. For DVULI purposes, this means a shared commitment to Christ and to the Core Values the program is built upon. Philosophy shapes method; misaligned philosophy produces frustration rather than growth.
  2. Subject knowledge with objective criticism. A mentor who simply says what the mentee wants to hear is, in Smith's striking phrase, "irresponsible." The mentor must be knowledgeable enough to evaluate accurately and honest enough to say so — including the phrase "I don't know" when appropriate.
  3. Genuine belief in the mentee's potential. Without this conviction, the mentor will not invest the cognitive and emotional energy the relationship demands. Crucially, this belief must survive the mentee's own failure of confidence — which will happen.
  4. Ability to help define vision, goal, and plan. Smith's metaphor is vivid: "I was talking to another man in the same circumstances and I said, 'You could have married six or eight young women but you chose one. You will have to do the same with your goal.'" Focus is a gift the mentor gives by helping the mentee choose one direction among the many possibilities that capable leaders always face.
  5. Good chemistry expressed through clear communication. Smith's test is direct: "Before I start work with someone, I will check this by talking a few minutes and then ask the person to repeat what I've said." If the communication is routinely misread in both directions, the relationship will produce confusion rather than clarity.
  6. The experience to develop options rather than decisions. A good mentor does not take over the decision-making process; the mentor expands the field of options from which the mentee makes their own informed choices. "A good mentor is not a quick-fix artist."
  7. Commitment to see the process through. Smith's image — "I don't want to get you halfway across the river" — captures a fundamental ethical obligation of the mentor relationship. Abandoning a mentee midway through a developmental season is a form of harm.
  8. Permission to hold the mentee accountable. The mentee must explicitly grant this permission; accountability cannot be imposed. Smith's analogy is memorable: "Accountability is like a tail on the kite — it keeps things from darting around."

Smith also outlines the responsibilities of the mentee, which are sometimes overlooked in the excitement of finding a good mentor. The effective mentee admits ignorance freely, works to ask the right questions by thinking carefully before speaking, does their homework before every meeting, never attempts to use the mentor for social capital or name-dropping, and grows visibly over time — because "progress is the pay the student gives the mentor." This is a demanding posture; it requires the kind of intellectual and spiritual humility that is genuinely countercultural in achievement-oriented ministry environments.

Think About It

Before you begin the mentor candidate process, reflect honestly: What has been your track record as a mentee? Have you brought prepared questions and done your homework? Have you made your growth visible to those who have invested in you? Or have previous mentoring relationships faded because you expected the mentor to drive the agenda? Smith's framework places the primary responsibility for the relationship's vitality on the mentee, not the mentor. How does that land for you?

Mentor Candidate Evaluator

Mentor Quality

The DVULI Core Values Mentor: Criteria and Process

The DVULI workbook specifies a set of non-negotiable criteria that a Core Values mentor must meet. These criteria are designed to ensure that the relationship actually serves the program's developmental purposes rather than simply providing pleasant conversation. The five core qualities, mapped to the rating scale you have just practiced, are: (1) a mature, committed Christian who will give honest feedback; (2) someone who demonstrably applies the Core Values in personal and professional life; (3) someone who has personally executed a strategic, significant, and sustained personal or career breakthrough — a breakthrough in the DVULI-defined sense; (4) someone who demonstrates proven leadership qualities; and (5) someone who is willing to attend a two-hour orientation and meet monthly in person for one year and possibly longer.

Additional structural requirements deserve emphasis. The mentor must be of the same gender. The mentor must not be a current supervisor, pastor, or family member. The mentor must be willing and able to commit to monthly face-to-face meetings — not phone calls or video conference as a substitute. And crucially: the mentor's most important gift to the mentee is their time. A candidate who leads a very busy life and struggles to return phone calls or maintain commitments is not well-suited to this role, regardless of how impressive their credentials may appear. Daloz (1999) notes that effective mentors do not need to be perfect models — they need to be honest companions. The most dangerous false criterion in mentor selection is prestige. A nationally recognized leader who has limited time and emotional bandwidth will serve a mentee less well than a committed local leader who genuinely believes in the mentee's potential and will actually show up.

The DVULI timeline for mentor engagement is specific: identify candidates before the end of this workshop, consult with your City Coordinator, invite the chosen candidate by providing them the "What Is a DVULI Mentor?" handout, secure their commitment prior to the First National Conference, and begin monthly meetings in May–June. The agenda for every meeting is the mentee's responsibility, not the mentor's. Begin with your goals related to the Core Values; return to those goals at each meeting; and be willing to be held accountable for what you commit to between sessions.

Reflective Journaling: The Discipline That Deepens Everything Else

The second sustaining discipline introduced in Session 4 is reflective journaling. Douglas Rumford's article "The Value of a Personal Journal," included in the Pre-Assignment, offers both the theological grounding and the practical framework for the kind of journaling DVULI asks participants to maintain. Rumford's opening image is striking: "How is it that when a person prays for illumination and receives it, he or she can let that precious truth slip away like the tide erasing writing in the sand?" The journal exists to catch what would otherwise be lost.

Schön's (1983) landmark work on professional practice provides the theoretical scaffolding for understanding what Rumford was intuiting pastorally. Schön distinguished between reflection-in-action (the kind of thinking we do while doing, adjusting in real time) and reflection-on-action (the retrospective sense-making we do after an experience has concluded). Expert practitioners in complex professions — and urban ministry is precisely such a profession — develop their competence not through the linear application of learned rules, but through iterative cycles of returning to experience with new data, revising their understanding each time they do so. Schön called this spiral movement the reflective loop: the practitioner does not simply move forward chronologically but returns, again and again, to earlier frames of understanding and finds them transformed by what has happened since.

Rumford names this same dynamic with his own term: spiraling. "This is my own term for going over the same ground again and again. The center of the spiral, the issue, may be the same, but our understanding of it is continually deepening and progressing like the widening loops of a spiral." The Personal Learning Journal you will maintain throughout DVULI and complete as part of your Breakthrough Plan is not a diary of events. It is not an academic paper. It is not a record of other people's ideas. The workbook is explicit: "In your journal, you are the only expert that matters." It is a structured space for reflecting intelligently on what and how you are learning, including how you are applying those lessons and what outcomes you are achieving as a result.

The DVULI two-column format for journaling is intentionally counterintuitive. The right column — labeled "Response to Events" — is written first and contemporaneously, capturing immediate reactions to learning experiences while they are still fresh. The left column — labeled "Reflective Loop: Deeper Insights" — is written later, after time has passed and new experiences have added data. This temporal gap is itself a pedagogical tool. Kolb (1984) showed that the movement from concrete experience to reflective observation to abstract conceptualization requires time and distance; compressing that movement eliminates the insight the cycle is designed to generate. The locked left column in the journal simulator below is not a technical trick; it is a discipline.

Rumford's practical principles for journaling are worth holding alongside Schön's theoretical framework: trust the Holy Spirit to guide each session; work with feelings and perceptions rather than a chronicle of dates; trust your own insights; be completely free and write for your eyes only; be honest — "don't fool yourself with pious talk"; and discipline yourself to eventually seek the promise, not just state the facts. The seventh principle, spiraling, is the most advanced and the most rewarding: returning to earlier entries with the eyes of new experience and finding them charged with meaning you could not have seen when you first wrote them.

The Harvest of Integration: Carrying Your Tree Forward

The Leadership Tree poster you complete in Session 4 is not an exercise you will file away. It is a living document — a reference point to which you will return in every subsequent session, every group dialogue, and every mentoring conversation. When you encounter the Core Values of Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage in the First National Conference, you will be evaluating them in relation to a specific leader — yourself — whose roots, trunk, and branches you have now named with precision. When you engage systems thinking in the Second National Conference, you will be examining the systems that either nourish or threaten the health of your specific tree. When you write your Breakthrough Plan, the roots section of your tree will be the foundation from which your Personal Focus Statement grows.

George and colleagues (2007) found that leaders with clear personal purpose and strong support networks — what they termed "true north groups" — consistently outperform and outlast leaders without them. They also identified what they called "crucible moments" — formative experiences that, when reflected upon honestly, reveal the core values and leadership purpose that have always been present beneath the surface. Your Leadership Tree is, in essence, a crucible document. It catches the insights that emerged from your crucible moments and gives them a structure through which they can be shared, tested, and built upon.

The Pre-Assignment workbook reflection questions ask you to consider what key insights you have gained and what questions the process has raised. Those are not idle journaling prompts; they are the beginning of a learning history that will accumulate over the next year and culminate in your Breakthrough Plan and oral presentation. Every time you enter a right-column reflection in your two-column journal and return later to discover a deeper layer in the left column, you are practicing the reflective loop that Schön identified as the hallmark of expert practitioners. Every time you show up to your monthly mentoring meeting with prepared questions and evidence of growth, you are modeling for the emerging leaders in your ministry what it looks like to be a learner rather than a knower.

The claim at the heart of Session 4 is simple but demanding: a healthy tree produces fruit over a very long time, but only if all three structural systems are intact and actively tended. Roots without trunk collapse under the weight of isolation. Trunk without roots become a hollow shell — supportive in appearance but nourishing nothing. Branches without roots and trunk are simply cut flowers: beautiful for a season, then dry. The DVULI program is making a long bet on your willingness to tend your whole tree — not just the parts of it that other people can see.

Key Takeaways

  • The Leadership Tree integrates your personal purpose (Roots), your support network (Trunk), and your DISC Natural Style plus LSA strategies (Branches/Leaves) into a single, portable portrait of your divine design.
  • Roots (purpose) supply the invisible nourishment from which all visible growth flows. A leader without a clear, living purpose will eventually drift no matter how impressive their programs appear.
  • The Trunk (people who support and pray for you) is the load-bearing structure that allows the leader to stand under pressure and grow sustainably; its health is entirely the leader's own responsibility to cultivate.
  • Branches and Leaves (DISC + LSA) reveal how you are currently showing up for those you lead — both your strengths and your growing edges, in the service of greater effectiveness and flexibility.
  • Effective mentoring (Fred Smith) requires compatibility of philosophy, honest feedback, belief in the mentee's potential, skill at defining vision and plan, clear chemistry, options rather than decisions, commitment, and explicit accountability.
  • The Core Values mentor must demonstrate a personal breakthrough in the DVULI sense, be of the same gender, be willing to meet monthly in person, and must not be a supervisor, family member, or current pastor.
  • Reflective journaling (Douglas Rumford / Donald Schön) operates through the spiral: immediate response in the right column, deepened insight in the left column, returned to again and again as new experience provides fresh lenses.
  • The two-column journal format is a deliberate discipline — the temporal gap between right and left columns is not incidental but essential to the learning the reflective loop is designed to generate.
  • Both mentoring and journaling are not supplemental activities but the primary disciplines through which DVULI learning is converted into breakthrough — the roots through which the whole program draws its deepest nourishment.

Looking Ahead

Session 4 completes the first arc of the DVULI program — the arc of self-knowledge. Beginning with the First National Conference and the introduction of the five Core Values (Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage), the program pivots from inward reflection to outward application. You will bring your Leadership Tree — now complete — into a world of systemic challenges, community assets, and strategic futures. The question shifts from "Who am I?" to "What will I do with who I am?" Come to the First National Conference having met with at least one potential mentor candidate and having completed at least two right-column journal entries in your two-column journal. The left column will be waiting.

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: The Leadership Tree: Roots (purpose), Trunk (support network), Branches/Leaves (DISC + LSA) as integrated leadership portrait
Mentoring That Matters: eight characteristics of effective mentor-mentee relationships (Fred Smith)
Reflective Journaling: the two-column reflective loop method and its disciplines (Douglas Rumford)
Identifying and engaging a Core Values mentor: criteria, candidate selection, timeline

items.question: In the Leadership Tree metaphor, what do the Roots represent?
items.correctFeedback: The Roots of the Leadership Tree represent the participant's personal purpose statement. Just as roots provide invisible nourishment that makes everything else possible in a real tree, a leader's purpose is the foundational source that sustains all other dimensions of their leadership.

distractors.text: The leader's DISC behavioral style
distractors.feedback: Students may confuse this because DISC is a foundational self-discovery tool in the program. However, DISC style is mapped to the Branches and Leaves of the tree — the visible, outward expressions of leadership — not the invisible nourishment of the Roots.

distractors.text: The leader's support network of trusted people
distractors.feedback: The support network is an important part of the Leadership Tree, but it corresponds to the Trunk, which provides structural support. The Roots specifically represent purpose — the invisible foundation beneath everything.

distractors.text: The leader's ministry programs and activities
distractors.feedback: Ministry programs and visible activities might seem foundational, but the session explicitly warns that many urban leaders have impressive visible activity (branches) while their roots (purpose) are compromised. The tree metaphor is about the leader themselves, not their programs.

items.question: According to the session, which part of the Leadership Tree provides the structural support without which no growth is sustainable?
items.correctAnswer: The Trunk, representing the people who support the leader
items.correctFeedback: The Trunk represents the leader's support network — the people who sustain them. Just as a tree's trunk provides the structural integrity connecting roots to branches, a leader's support community provides the relational infrastructure that makes continued growth possible.

distractors.text: The Roots, representing the leader's theological training
distractors.feedback: While the Roots do provide foundational nourishment, they represent the leader's personal purpose statement, not theological training. The structural support role belongs specifically to the Trunk and its representation of the support network.

distractors.text: The Branches, representing the leader's ministry partnerships
distractors.feedback: The Branches represent DISC style and LSA strategies — the visible expressions of leadership — not ministry partnerships. Students may confuse relational elements, but the support network is specifically mapped to the Trunk.

distractors.text: The Leaves, representing the leader's spiritual disciplines
distractors.feedback: The Leaves, along with Branches, represent DISC style and LSA strategies — the outward expressions others observe. Spiritual disciplines are not a named component of the Leadership Tree metaphor as presented in this session.

items.question: The session identifies a common pattern among urban ministry leaders. Which diagnosis does the Leadership Tree metaphor help reveal?
items.correctAnswer: Impressive branches with compromised roots and an insufficient trunk
items.correctFeedback: The session warns that many urban ministry leaders display impressive visible activity (branches) while suffering from unclear or forgotten purpose (compromised roots) and absent or inadequate support networks (insufficient trunk). The Leadership Tree helps participants honestly assess which parts are thriving and which are struggling.

distractors.text: Strong roots and trunk but underdeveloped branches and leaves
distractors.feedback: While this could theoretically describe some leaders, the session specifically identifies the opposite pattern as the common danger: leaders who are visibly active but whose foundational purpose and support structures are weak.

distractors.text: Equally balanced development across all parts of the tree
distractors.feedback: If leaders had equally balanced development, there would be little need for the diagnostic exercise. The whole point of the Leadership Tree is to surface imbalances — and the session specifically names the pattern of strong branches with weak roots and trunk as the common problem.

distractors.text: Overgrown roots that prevent the branches from receiving nourishment
distractors.feedback: This reverses the actual diagnosis. The session does not describe over-developed purpose as a problem. Rather, the concern is leaders whose purpose is unclear or forgotten (underdeveloped roots), not leaders whose purpose is too strong.

items.question: The Leadership Tree metaphor is explicitly described as a metaphor for which of the following?
items.correctAnswer: The leader themselves as a person
items.correctFeedback: The session makes a deliberate clarifying statement: the tree is not a metaphor for ambitious ministry programs — it is a metaphor for the leader themselves. This distinction is critical because it shifts the diagnostic focus from evaluating organizational output to evaluating personal health and sustainability.

distractors.text: The leader's ministry organization and its programs
distractors.feedback: The session explicitly distinguishes the Leadership Tree from a ministry-program metaphor. Students who focus on organizational health may assume the tree represents the ministry, but the text specifically states it represents the leader as a person.

distractors.text: The DVULI workshop curriculum structure
distractors.feedback: While the Leadership Tree is the DVULI's signature summary tool, it does not represent the curriculum structure. It represents the individual leader and integrates three streams of self-discovery into a single portrait.

distractors.text: The mentor-mentee relationship and its growth over time
distractors.feedback: Mentoring is introduced in the same session but is a separate discipline from the Leadership Tree. The tree represents the leader's integrated profile — purpose, support, and style — not the mentoring relationship.

items.question: Which combination of self-discovery streams are synthesized together on the Leadership Tree poster?
items.correctAnswer: Purpose statement, DISC style, and LSA strategies
items.correctFeedback: The Leadership Tree is described as the first major synthesis point where all three prior streams of self-discovery — purpose (Roots), DISC behavioral style, and LSA leadership strategies (Branches/Leaves) — flow together into a single integrated portrait, along with the support network (Trunk).

distractors.text: Reflective journaling entries, mentor feedback, and DISC style
distractors.feedback: Reflective journaling and mentoring are introduced in the same session but as forward-looking disciplines, not as prior self-discovery streams being synthesized. The three streams synthesized are purpose, DISC, and LSA.

distractors.text: Core values assessment, spiritual gifts inventory, and purpose statement
distractors.feedback: While core values and purpose are related concepts, the session specifically names DISC and LSA as two of the three self-discovery streams. A spiritual gifts inventory is not identified as one of the synthesized streams in the Leadership Tree.

distractors.text: Fred Smith's mentoring principles, DISC style, and personal purpose
distractors.feedback: Fred Smith's mentoring principles are introduced in the second half of the session as a separate discipline. The three streams flowing into the Leadership Tree are purpose, DISC, and LSA — all based on pre-assignment data.

items.question: According to Fred Smith's 'Mentoring That Matters,' how many characteristics of effective mentor-mentee relationships are identified?
items.correctFeedback: Fred Smith's framework identifies eight characteristics of effective mentors and also outlines the responsibilities of the mentee. These characteristics form the criteria participants use when evaluating potential mentor candidates for their Core Values mentor selection.

distractors.text: Five characteristics
distractors.feedback: Students may guess a round number or confuse this with other frameworks in the program. Fred Smith's model specifically outlines eight characteristics, which is a distinctive feature of his mentoring framework.

distractors.text: Ten characteristics
distractors.feedback: Ten is a common number for lists and frameworks, but Fred Smith's model identifies exactly eight characteristics. Overestimating the number suggests conflation with other models or lists encountered in the course.

distractors.text: Twelve characteristics
distractors.feedback: Students may inflate the number by combining mentor characteristics with mentee responsibilities into one list. The framework distinguishes between the eight characteristics of effective mentors and the separate responsibilities of the mentee.

items.question: Douglas Rumford's reflective journaling method includes a practice called 'spiraling.' What does this practice involve?
items.correctAnswer: Returning to earlier journal entries with fresh insight gained over time
items.correctFeedback: Spiraling is the practice of revisiting previous journal entries and adding new perspectives, connections, or insights that have emerged since the original writing. This creates a reflective loop rather than a linear diary, allowing the journal to become a dynamic tool for deepening self-understanding across the full arc of the program.

distractors.text: Writing in a circular pattern on the page to stimulate creative thinking
distractors.feedback: This is a literal interpretation of the word 'spiraling' but misunderstands the concept. Spiraling refers to a temporal practice — returning to earlier entries over time — not a physical writing pattern on the page.

distractors.text: Gradually expanding journal entries from brief notes to full essays
distractors.feedback: While expanding on ideas could be part of the journaling process, spiraling specifically refers to the practice of returning to earlier entries with fresh insight, not simply making entries longer. The emphasis is on the reflective loop, not on entry length.

distractors.text: Alternating between personal reflection and peer-shared journaling sessions
distractors.feedback: The spiraling practice is an individual reflective discipline, not a group activity. It refers to the internal process of revisiting one's own earlier entries with new understanding, not to alternating between private and shared writing.

items.question: A participant completes her Leadership Tree and notices she has a clearly articulated purpose statement and strong DISC/LSA awareness, but she can identify very few people in her support network. Using the tree metaphor, what is the most accurate diagnosis of her leadership health?
items.correctAnswer: Her tree has healthy roots and branches but a weak trunk, making it structurally vulnerable
items.correctFeedback: A clear purpose statement maps to healthy Roots, and strong DISC/LSA awareness maps to healthy Branches and Leaves. However, a minimal support network means her Trunk is underdeveloped. Without structural support, even a tree with strong roots and lush branches is vulnerable to collapse — the session's central claim is that damage to any part of the tree threatens the whole leader.

distractors.text: Her tree is fundamentally healthy because purpose is the most important component
distractors.feedback: While purpose (Roots) is foundational, the session's central claim is that a tree with damaged or underdeveloped roots, trunk, OR branches soon dies. No single component can compensate for weakness in another. An absent support network is a serious vulnerability.

distractors.text: Her tree has weak roots because a support network is what nourishes the leader
distractors.feedback: This confuses the metaphorical roles. Roots represent purpose and provide nourishment; the Trunk represents the support network and provides structural support. Her roots (purpose) are actually healthy — it is her trunk (support) that is weak.

distractors.text: Her tree needs pruning because she has over-invested in self-awareness at the expense of relationships
distractors.feedback: Pruning is not a diagnostic category in the Leadership Tree framework. The issue is not over-investment in one area but under-development of the Trunk. The tree metaphor diagnoses the health of each component rather than prescribing reduction of healthy parts.

items.question: The two-column reflective loop method used in the DVULI journaling discipline is designed to cultivate which primary capacity in leaders?
items.correctAnswer: The ability to extract deeper meaning from experiences through structured reflection
items.correctFeedback: The two-column reflective loop method creates a disciplined structure for moving beyond surface-level description of events to deeper analysis and personal insight. By pairing observations with reflections, participants develop the habit of learning from their experiences — a capacity that culminates in the Personal Learning Journal within the Breakthrough Plan.

distractors.text: The ability to document daily ministry activities for accountability reporting
distractors.feedback: While journaling does create a record, the reflective loop method is not designed for activity documentation or accountability. Its purpose is to cultivate deeper meaning-making, not administrative tracking. Reducing it to reporting misses the reflective dimension entirely.

distractors.text: The ability to write persuasive arguments for ministry funding proposals
distractors.feedback: Persuasive writing is a different skill entirely. Reflective journaling is an inward-facing discipline designed to deepen self-understanding, not an outward-facing communication skill for external audiences.

distractors.text: The ability to summarize readings and lectures for future reference
distractors.feedback: Summarizing content is a study skill, not a reflective practice. The two-column reflective loop method emphasizes the participant's own experiences and insights, not content retention. The spiraling practice of returning to entries distinguishes it from a simple note-taking system.

items.question: When selecting a Core Values mentor, participants are instructed to follow a process that includes identifying criteria, selecting candidates, and establishing a timeline. Why does the DVULI build all three elements into the mentor selection process rather than simply asking participants to find a mentor?
items.correctAnswer: Because a structured process increases the likelihood of finding a mentor who genuinely fits the participant's developmental needs
items.correctFeedback: The three-part process — criteria, candidate selection, and timeline — ensures that mentor selection is intentional rather than haphazard. Defining criteria based on Fred Smith's characteristics helps participants evaluate fit; identifying multiple candidates prevents over-dependence on one option; and setting a timeline creates accountability for follow-through. Together these elements transform mentoring from a vague aspiration into a concrete, developmentally aligned commitment.

distractors.text: Because the DVULI requires formal documentation of the mentor selection for program accreditation
distractors.feedback: While the DVULI may track mentor engagement, the rationale for the structured process is developmental, not administrative. The session frames the process as serving the participant's growth, not as satisfying a bureaucratic requirement.

distractors.text: Because participants are expected to mentor others simultaneously, requiring careful time management
distractors.feedback: The session focuses on participants as mentees seeking a Core Values mentor, not on participants mentoring others at this stage. The structured process is about selecting the right mentor for their own development, not about managing dual mentoring roles.

distractors.text: Because the mentor must be approved by DVULI program staff before the relationship can begin
distractors.feedback: The session describes the process as participant-driven, with criteria drawn from Fred Smith's principles. The structure exists to empower thoughtful, personally meaningful selection — not to create an external approval mechanism.

questions.stem: In the Leadership Tree metaphor, what do the Roots represent?
options: The leader's personal purpose statement
The leader's DISC behavioral style
The leader's support network of relationships
The leader's visible ministry activities

feedback.correct: The Roots represent the leader's personal purpose statement — the invisible nourishment that makes everything else possible. Just as a tree's roots are hidden underground yet essential for life, a leader's purpose is often unseen by others but supplies the foundational energy for all leadership activity.
feedback.incorrect: Many confuse the Roots with more visible leadership elements. Remember the metaphor's logic: roots are underground and invisible. The leader's purpose statement operates the same way — it's not always visible to others, but it nourishes everything above the surface.

variants.stem: Which part of the Leadership Tree provides what the session describes as 'the invisible nourishment that makes everything else possible'?
options: The Roots
The Trunk
The Branches
The Leaves

feedback.correct: The Roots provide the invisible nourishment — they represent the leader's personal purpose statement. Like a biological tree's root system, purpose is hidden from view but is the essential source of life and vitality for the entire leadership structure.
feedback.incorrect: The key word is 'invisible.' The Trunk, Branches, and Leaves are all visible parts of a tree. The Roots are the only component that operates beneath the surface, which is why they map to purpose — something deeply personal and often unseen by others.

questions.stem: According to the Leadership Tree model, which component represents the people who provide structural support to the leader?
options: The Trunk
The Roots
The Branches
The Leaves

feedback.correct: The Trunk represents the leader's support network — mentors, accountability partners, and other key relationships. Just as a tree's trunk provides the rigid structural support that holds the branches aloft, a leader's support community provides the relational infrastructure that makes sustained ministry possible.
feedback.incorrect: Think about the physical function of each tree part. The trunk is the structural backbone that connects roots to branches. In the Leadership Tree, this structural role belongs to the people who support the leader — without them, the branches (visible activity) would collapse.

variants.stem: A participant mapping their Leadership Tree would place their mentor, accountability partners, and key supporters in which section?
options: The Trunk
The Roots
The Branches
The Leaves

feedback.correct: Mentors, accountability partners, and supporters are all placed in the Trunk. The Trunk represents the support network — the relational structure that holds the leader up and connects their hidden purpose (Roots) to their visible activity (Branches and Leaves).
feedback.incorrect: Supporters and mentors are not part of the Roots (purpose), Branches (DISC style), or Leaves (LSA strategies). They form the Trunk — the structural support that keeps the whole tree standing. Without a strong Trunk, even leaders with clear purpose and great skill will eventually topple.

questions.stem: Pastor Maria leads a thriving after-school program, speaks regularly at community events, and is widely regarded as an energetic leader. However, she recently admitted to a colleague that she can no longer articulate why she does what she does. Using the Leadership Tree framework, what is the most accurate diagnosis of her situation?
options: She has impressive Branches but compromised Roots
She has a strong Trunk but weak Branches
She has healthy Roots but an underdeveloped Trunk
She has balanced growth across all components
justifications: Her visible ministry activity (Branches) is flourishing, but her inability to articulate purpose indicates her Roots are damaged — the Leadership Tree framework warns this pattern will eventually threaten the health of the entire tree
Her speaking engagements prove strong relational support, while her confusion shows she needs more diverse ministry programs
Her busyness demonstrates that her purpose is intact since only purposeful people stay active, but she needs more supporters
All leaders go through periodic confusion, and this simply represents a normal growth season that the tree metaphor does not address

feedback.correct: Maria exemplifies the pattern the session explicitly warns about: many urban ministry leaders have impressive Branches (visible activity) with compromised Roots (unclear or forgotten purpose). The Leadership Tree framework insists that a tree with damaged roots — no matter how lush its branches appear — will eventually die.
feedback.incorrect: Being busy and visible does not mean a leader's Roots are healthy. The Roots represent purpose, not activity. Maria's inability to articulate her 'why' is a Root-level problem, even though her Branch-level output looks strong. The session warns that this mismatch is dangerously common.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes correctly identify the Branches-Roots mismatch but misattribute it to a temporary season rather than a structural problem. The Leadership Tree framework treats Root compromise as an existential threat to the tree, not a passing phase.

variants.stem: Youth leader DeShawn has built an impressive network of volunteers and community partners who deeply support his work. His programs are modest in scope, and when asked about his life mission, he gives a vague, uncertain answer. Using the Leadership Tree framework, what is the most accurate diagnosis?
options: He has a strong Trunk but compromised Roots and underdeveloped Branches
He has impressive Branches but a weak Trunk
He has healthy Roots driving his relational investments
He has a fully balanced and healthy Leadership Tree
justifications: His volunteer network represents a strong Trunk (support), but his vague mission indicates compromised Roots (purpose), and modest programs suggest underdeveloped Branches — the tree has structural support but lacks both nourishment and visible canopy
His relational network proves his purpose is clear because only purposeful leaders attract supporters
Modest programs are always a sign of weak supporters, not unclear purpose
The Leadership Tree only diagnoses Roots and Branches, so trunk strength is irrelevant to the overall assessment

feedback.correct: DeShawn's profile shows an unusual pattern: a strong Trunk (support network) without strong Roots (purpose) or Branches (visible style expression). The Leadership Tree framework would say his supporters are holding him up, but without clear purpose feeding the system, sustainable growth is unlikely.
feedback.incorrect: It's tempting to assume that strong relationships mean strong purpose, but the Leadership Tree separates these into different components for a reason. A leader can attract supporters through personality or proximity without having a clear personal purpose driving the whole system.
feedback.wrongReason: Some students correctly identify the Trunk strength but fail to distinguish between Roots and Branches, treating all non-Trunk elements as a single category. The framework insists that purpose (Roots) and style expression (Branches) are distinct components that must each be assessed independently.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: The DVULI program requires participants to identify and engage a Core Values mentor themselves rather than assigning one to them.
questions.reason: Fred Smith's principles emphasize that the mentee bears significant responsibility for initiating and sustaining the mentoring relationship.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and Smith's principle directly explains the program's design. Because effective mentoring depends on mentee initiative, the DVULI intentionally structures the process so that participants practice that initiative from the very start — selecting, approaching, and engaging their own mentor rather than passively receiving one.
feedback.incorrect: A common error is seeing these as independently true facts. Consider the causal logic: if the DVULI believes mentees should take initiative (Smith's principle), it would be contradictory for the program to assign mentors for them. The design of the program (participant-driven selection) flows directly from the principle (mentee responsibility).

variants.assertion: The Leadership Tree places the support network in the Trunk rather than in the Roots or Branches.
variants.reason: A tree's trunk is the most visible part, and a leader's support network is the most publicly observable element of their leadership.

feedback.correct: The assertion is true — the support network is indeed placed in the Trunk. However, the reason is false. The Trunk is chosen not because it is the most visible part, but because it provides structural support connecting Roots to Branches. In fact, the most visible parts of a tree are its Branches and Leaves, not its Trunk.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes assume that if the assertion is true, the reason must explain it. But visibility is not why the Trunk was chosen for the support network. The Branches and Leaves are actually described as the 'visible, outward expressions' of leadership. The Trunk's metaphorical role is structural support, not visibility.

questions.stem: Douglas Rumford's reflective journaling method involves a 'spiraling' practice. What does this practice specifically require the journaler to do?
options: Return to earlier journal entries and add fresh insights gained over time
Write each entry in an increasingly deeper level of emotional vulnerability
Alternate between personal reflection and group discussion in a repeating cycle
Complete one full journal before starting a new one on the same topics

feedback.correct: Rumford's spiraling practice is the discipline of revisiting earlier entries with the benefit of new experience and perspective. This 'two-column reflective loop' creates layered understanding over time — what you wrote three months ago takes on new meaning when you return to it with fresh eyes, creating a deepening spiral of insight.
feedback.incorrect: The spiraling metaphor refers specifically to the practice of physically returning to previous journal entries and annotating them with new insights. It is not about emotional depth, group sharing, or sequential journals. The power of the method is in the re-reading and re-interpreting of your own earlier thinking.

variants.stem: What is the defining feature of the 'two-column reflective loop' method introduced through Douglas Rumford's journaling principles?
options: One column captures the original reflection while a second column captures later insights when revisiting the entry
One column lists problems encountered while the other column lists biblical passages for guidance
One column records the leader's perspective while the other records their mentor's perspective
One column contains facts about a situation while the other contains the leader's DISC-informed response

feedback.correct: The two-column method creates a structured space for the spiraling practice: the original entry lives in one column, and when the journaler returns to that entry later, fresh insights are captured in the second column. This architecture makes growth visible over time and cultivates the discipline of revisiting one's own thinking.
feedback.incorrect: The two-column structure is not about pairing problems with Scripture, self with mentor, or facts with style. It is a temporal structure — original reflection paired with later reflection on the same material. The power is in seeing how your own understanding has changed over time.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about the Leadership Tree framework as 'Always true,' 'Sometimes true,' or 'Never true':

statements.text: The Roots of the Leadership Tree are visible to those the leader serves
A leader's DISC behavioral style is displayed in the Branches and Leaves of their Leadership Tree
A leader with strong visible ministry activity but no clear purpose has a healthy Leadership Tree
The Trunk of a Leadership Tree can be strengthened by identifying and engaging a mentor
A leader's Leadership Tree poster remains unchanged throughout the entire DVULI program
LSA preferred strategies appear in the Roots section of the Leadership Tree
A participant with a clear purpose, strong support network, and well-understood DISC/LSA profile can still have growth areas in their Leadership Tree

feedback.correct: The key distinctions center on the fixed mapping: Roots always equal purpose (invisible), Trunk always equals support (structural), and Branches/Leaves always equal DISC and LSA (visible). Understanding which elements are always true versus sometimes true requires grasping both the metaphor's logic and the program's developmental arc.
feedback.incorrect: The most common confusion involves boundary cases — especially whether the tree is static or dynamic, and whether strong components in one area can compensate for weakness in another. The framework insists all three must be healthy (no compensation), but the tree does develop over time as leaders grow in self-awareness.

variants.stem: Classify each statement about mentoring and the Leadership Tree as 'Always true,' 'Sometimes true,' or 'Never true':

statements.text: The mentee bears responsibility for initiating the mentoring relationship
A mentor must share the same DISC behavioral style as the mentee to be effective
Reflective journaling entries will be relevant to the participant's Breakthrough Plan
A leader with a strong Trunk can compensate for weak Roots indefinitely
The same person can serve as both a Trunk supporter and a Core Values mentor
A participant's Leadership Tree reveals areas of strength alongside areas needing development

feedback.correct: The critical distinctions test whether components can substitute for each other (they cannot), whether mentoring has rigid stylistic requirements (it does not), and whether tools like journaling connect to later program deliverables (they do). The framework resists the temptation to let one strong area excuse neglect of another.
feedback.incorrect: Common errors include assuming that strong support can replace unclear purpose, or that mentoring requires stylistic similarity. The Leadership Tree framework insists each component has a unique function that cannot be replaced by another, and Fred Smith's mentoring principles focus on character and commitment, not personality match.

questions.stem: According to Fred Smith's 'Mentoring That Matters,' how many characteristics of effective mentor-mentee relationships are identified?

feedback.correct: Fred Smith identifies exactly eight characteristics of effective mentor-mentee relationships. These characteristics cover both the mentor's qualities and the mentee's responsibilities, forming a comprehensive framework that participants use to evaluate potential mentor candidates and prepare themselves to be effective mentees.
feedback.incorrect: Students often overestimate this number, assuming that a comprehensive mentoring framework must have ten or more elements. Others underestimate, conflating the full list with just the two or three characteristics they remember most. Smith intentionally identified eight — enough to be thorough without being overwhelming.

variants.stem: How many distinct streams of prior self-discovery work are integrated together in the Leadership Tree poster during Session 4?

feedback.correct: Three prior streams of self-discovery converge in the Leadership Tree: the personal purpose statement (from Session 1), the DISC behavioral profile (from Session 2), and the LSA leadership strategies (from Session 3). These three streams, developed independently, are synthesized into a single integrated leadership portrait for the first time.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes count additional elements like the support network or the mentor as separate 'streams,' arriving at four or five. While these are displayed on the poster, the session specifically identifies three prior streams of self-discovery (purpose, DISC, LSA) as the inputs being integrated. The support network is a current-session addition, not a prior stream.

questions.stem: Keisha completes her Leadership Tree poster and sees the following profile: her Roots show a clear purpose centered on empowering at-risk youth through education; her Trunk reveals only two supporters, both from her own church; her Branches show a high-I (Influencing) DISC style with a preference for the Mentoring LSA strategy. She is now asked to identify a Core Values mentor and begin reflective journaling. Which integrated plan best addresses the full picture of her Leadership Tree while properly applying Fred Smith's mentoring principles and Rumford's journaling method?
options: Seek a mentor outside her church who has experience in youth education, using her high-I relational strengths to build the relationship, while journaling in a two-column format to track how her thin Trunk affects her sustainability
Choose one of her two existing church supporters as her mentor since they already know her, and journal exclusively about her purpose statement to keep her Roots strong
Focus her journaling on adapting her DISC style from Influencing to Conscientiousness since detail-orientation is her weakness, and delay mentor selection until her Branches improve
Recruit several mentees immediately since her Mentoring LSA strategy is her strength, which will simultaneously expand her Trunk and demonstrate her purpose
justifications: This plan addresses her Trunk gap (seeking a mentor outside her limited church network), leverages her DISC strength (high-I leaders excel at building new relationships), applies Smith's mentoring criteria (experience alignment with her purpose area), and uses Rumford's two-column spiral method to reflect on the sustainability risk her thin support network creates
Choosing an existing supporter is most efficient because trust already exists, and journaling about purpose keeps the strongest part of the tree healthy
DISC style adaptation is the highest priority because leaders must overcome their weaknesses before they can mentor others effectively
The Mentoring LSA strategy means she should be mentoring others, and finding mentees is the fastest way to build relationships that double as support

feedback.correct: The best plan weaves together all three session disciplines. It uses the Leadership Tree diagnosis (thin Trunk) to direct the mentor search, applies the DISC profile (high-I) as a resource for relationship-building rather than something to fix, respects Fred Smith's criteria by seeking a mentor with relevant expertise, and deploys Rumford's journaling method to create an ongoing reflective loop about the most vulnerable part of her tree.
feedback.incorrect: Partial plans fail because they address only one component. Choosing an existing supporter ignores Smith's principle that mentor selection should be deliberate, not convenient. Focusing on DISC weaknesses contradicts the session's message that Branches represent your design, not deficiencies. Recruiting mentees confuses the LSA Mentoring strategy (a leadership approach with followers) with receiving mentoring (a developmental relationship with a more experienced guide).
feedback.wrongReason: Students who pick the correct answer but wrong justification often get the practical steps right while misunderstanding why those steps work. The most common error is choosing the justification that focuses only on the Trunk gap without explaining how DISC style, Smith's criteria, and Rumford's method each contribute to the integrated plan.

variants.stem: Marcus completes his Leadership Tree poster and sees this profile: his Roots show a somewhat vague purpose related to 'helping people'; his Trunk shows a robust network of five supporters across three organizations; his Branches show a high-C (Conscientiousness) DISC style with a preference for the Instructing LSA strategy. He must now identify a Core Values mentor and begin reflective journaling. Which integrated plan best addresses the full picture of his Leadership Tree while properly applying the session's mentoring and journaling disciplines?
options: Use the two-column journal to systematically refine his vague purpose statement, and seek a mentor whose own clear sense of purpose can model what strong Roots look like — leveraging his existing Trunk relationships for mentor referrals
Select a mentor who shares his high-C style so they communicate naturally, and journal about his LSA Instructing strategy to make his Branches even stronger
Ask all five of his current supporters to co-mentor him since his Trunk is his strongest asset and more mentoring input is always better
Focus his journaling on building new relationships since his Trunk is already strong enough and his real gap is in the Branches
justifications: This plan targets his weakest component (vague Roots) through both journaling (using Rumford's reflective loop to sharpen purpose) and mentor selection (choosing someone who models clear purpose), while leveraging his strongest component (robust Trunk) as a practical resource for identifying mentor candidates
Matching mentor DISC styles ensures communication compatibility, which is the primary criterion for effective mentoring according to Fred Smith
Having five supporters means he has an unusually strong foundation, and distributing mentoring across all of them maximizes the benefit of his strongest tree component
His Roots are described as somewhat vague, which is actually normal and not a concern — the real priority should always be strengthening visible Branches

feedback.correct: Marcus's plan should prioritize his weakest component (vague Roots) while leveraging his strongest (robust Trunk). Rumford's two-column journal gives him a structured method to iteratively sharpen his purpose over time, and choosing a mentor who models clear purpose addresses the same gap from a relational direction. Using his existing network for referrals is both practical and smart.
feedback.incorrect: Common errors include assuming that a strong Trunk compensates for weak Roots (it cannot — the tree framework insists all components must be healthy), confusing DISC compatibility with mentor effectiveness (Smith's criteria are about character and experience, not personality match), and dismissing a vague purpose as normal rather than recognizing it as the Leadership Tree's most critical vulnerability.
feedback.wrongReason: Students who choose the right plan but wrong justification often miss the integration logic. The correct justification must explain why journaling addresses Roots, why the mentor selection criteria target purpose-modeling, and why the existing Trunk serves as a practical resource — all three elements matter, not just one.

questions.stem: According to the Week 1 framework, a leader's three-fold personal purpose involves knowing God, making the world better, and which third element?
options: Using your greatest gift
Building the largest ministry possible
Eliminating your greatest weakness
Following your denomination's strategic plan

feedback.correct: The three-fold purpose framework identifies knowing God, making the world better, and using your greatest gift as the three streams of personal purpose. This third element is critical because it grounds purpose in each individual's unique design rather than in a generic template — your purpose is shaped by what you specifically bring to the table.
feedback.incorrect: The framework intentionally focuses on gifts rather than weaknesses, institutional plans, or scale of impact. Purpose is personal, not organizational. The third element — using your greatest gift — connects directly to the Leadership Tree's Roots because it makes purpose specific to the individual leader rather than a one-size-fits-all formula.

variants.stem: The Personal Assessment Model from Week 1 identifies three inputs — Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change — that produce three outcomes. Which of the following is one of those three outcomes?
options: Teachability
Visibility
Productivity
Popularity

feedback.correct: The Personal Assessment Model shows that Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change produce Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability. Teachability is especially important for the DVULI because the entire program depends on leaders being willing to grow, learn, and be changed by new insights and honest feedback.
feedback.incorrect: The three outcomes — Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability — are all inner qualities of the leader, not external metrics. Visibility, productivity, and popularity may result from good leadership, but the model focuses on the character-level outcomes that make a leader genuinely ready for growth.

questions.stem: A youth pastor's DISC assessment reveals that her Natural Style is high-S (Steadiness) but her Adapted Style is high-D (Dominance). What does this gap most likely indicate?
options: She is adjusting her naturally relational, steady approach to meet the demands of her current ministry environment
She has permanently changed her core personality through years of ministry experience
Her Natural Style assessment was inaccurate and should be retaken for reliability
She is equally comfortable in both styles and can switch between them without personal cost
justifications: The gap between Natural and Adapted styles reveals behavioral adjustments a person makes to fit their current context, which can produce stress if sustained because they are performing outside their natural comfort zone
DISC styles are fixed at birth and cannot change, so any gap simply reflects a testing error in one of the two measures
People with high-S Natural styles are biologically unable to display Dominance behaviors, so the Adapted score must be artificially inflated
The Adapted Style represents what the person wishes they were like rather than any actual behavioral change in their current environment

feedback.correct: The Natural-to-Adapted gap is one of the most practically useful features of the DISC. It reveals the behavioral 'stretch' a leader is making to meet environmental demands.
