A high-S person adapting to high-D is working hard to be more directive and decisive than comes naturally — this is sustainable short-term but can be draining over time.
feedback.incorrect: The DISC does not claim styles are permanently fixed, nor does it suggest the assessment is unreliable when a gap appears. The gap is itself the insight: it shows where a leader is adapting, which has real implications for stress, sustainability, and self-awareness.
feedback.wrongReason: Students who get the right answer but wrong justification often recognize the adaptation but miss the cost. The correct justification specifically notes that sustained adaptation outside one's natural style produces stress — this is not neutral flexibility but an active expenditure of energy that leaders must manage intentionally.

variants.stem: A community outreach director's DISC profile shows a high-C (Conscientiousness) Natural Style but a high-I (Influencing) Adapted Style. What does this pattern most likely reveal?
options: He is adapting from his naturally analytical, detail-oriented approach to be more outgoing and persuasive in his current role
He has two equally dominant styles that he uses interchangeably without preference
His Adapted Style is aspirational and does not reflect any real behavioral change
The assessment is invalid because Conscientiousness and Influencing are opposite styles that cannot coexist
justifications: The Natural-to-Adapted gap shows he is stretching toward Influencing behaviors (public engagement, persuasion) that his community-facing role demands, even though his natural preference is for careful analysis — a common and meaningful adaptation for outreach leaders
Having opposite Natural and Adapted styles proves the assessment is measuring two different constructs that cannot be compared
High-C individuals cannot learn Influencing behaviors because the styles are neurologically incompatible
The Adapted Style is only relevant for job interviews and does not reflect daily behavioral patterns

feedback.correct: A high-C adapting to high-I is a classic pattern for detail-oriented people in public-facing roles. The Natural-Adapted gap reveals that his outreach position requires him to be more socially engaging than comes naturally. Understanding this gap helps him manage energy, seek support, and avoid burnout from sustained adaptation.
feedback.incorrect: Opposite styles in Natural versus Adapted profiles are not errors — they are among the most informative patterns the DISC produces. They reveal the magnitude of behavioral stretch a leader is making, which has direct implications for stress management and sustainable ministry.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes recognize the adaptation correctly but choose a justification that treats opposite styles as invalid or impossible. The DISC framework explicitly accounts for large Natural-Adapted gaps and treats them as valuable diagnostic information, not anomalies.

questions.stem: In the LSA framework, a team member who has developed low-to-some skill and has lost their initial enthusiasm — feeling discouraged about their progress — is at which development step?
options: Apprentice
Novice
Journeyman
Master

feedback.correct: The Apprentice stage is characterized by some emerging skill paired with declining dedication. The initial excitement of the Novice phase has worn off, reality has set in, and the person often feels discouraged. This is the 'wall' that many people hit after the honeymoon period, and it requires a Developing leadership strategy that balances direction with emotional support.
feedback.incorrect: Novices have high dedication but low skill — they're enthusiastic beginners. Journeymen have growing skill but variable dedication. Masters have both high skill and high dedication. The key marker of the Apprentice stage is the specific combination of some skill growth paired with a drop in motivation and confidence.

variants.stem: A volunteer who joined the ministry team two years ago now has strong skills and consistently high motivation — she takes initiative, solves problems independently, and trains newer team members. Which development step best describes her?
options: Master
Journeyman
Apprentice
Novice

feedback.correct: The Master stage is defined by the combination of high skill and high dedication. This volunteer demonstrates both: she has developed strong competence over two years and shows consistent motivation through initiative, problem-solving, and mentoring others. The appropriate LSA response is the Commissioning strategy — high trust, low direction.
feedback.incorrect: The development steps are defined by the intersection of skill and dedication. A Journeyman has moderate-high skill but variable commitment. An Apprentice has some skill but low motivation. A Novice has low skill but high enthusiasm. Only the Master combines both high skill and consistently high dedication.

prompts.prompt: Think of the best or worst mentor/boss you've ever had. What's the ONE thing that made them stand out — good or bad?
Would you rather have a mentor who tells you the hard truth and it stings, or one who's always in your corner but goes easy on you?
Be honest: do you actually know what your core values are — or is that just something that sounds good on a résumé?
A great leader with zero self-awareness vs. an average leader who really knows themselves — who causes more damage?
Do you think journaling and self-reflection actually make you better at leading people, or is it just glorified overthinking?
If someone asked you RIGHT NOW why you want to lead — not what you want to do, but WHY — could you answer in 30 seconds?

activities.title: Build Your Tree: Live Leadership Portrait Gallery
activities.description: Before the session, students are prompted to open a shared digital canvas (Miro, Jamboard, or a platform-native whiteboard) where a simple tree outline template is pre-loaded — one per student, tiled across the board. During the activity, students spend 8 minutes populating their own tree: Roots (their core purpose statement in one sentence), Trunk (2-3 people currently in their support network), and Branches/Leaves (their top DISC tendency and one LSA insight). Once populated, the instructor opens 7 minutes of 'gallery walk' time — students scroll the shared board and leave one sticky-note reaction on someone else's tree using a sentence starter like 'Your roots connect to your branches because...' or 'I see a pattern in your tree that...' The debrief asks 2-3 volunteers to read aloud what someone else wrote about their tree, turning peer observation into a moment of affirmation and insight.
activities.materials: Pre-built tree template on Miro/Jamboard or platform whiteboard; students need completed DISC + LSA results prior to session
activities.learningGoal: Synthesizes the Leadership Tree metaphor into a personal, integrated leadership portrait and builds peer recognition of leadership identity
activities.scalingNotes: For groups under 15, every student can share briefly. For groups over 30, limit gallery walk to a designated 'row' or cluster of 6-8 trees per student to keep it manageable.

activities.title: The Mentor Matchmaker: Fred Smith's Eight in Action
activities.description: Students are placed into breakout rooms of 4-5. Each room receives a shared Google Doc or platform document with two columns: on the left, a scenario describing a fictional mentor-mentee relationship in one paragraph; on the right, a blank rubric listing Fred Smith's eight characteristics of effective mentoring. Groups spend 8 minutes reading the scenario and debating which characteristics are present, which are absent, and what specific evidence from the scenario supports their rating. One twist: each group gets a slightly different scenario (effective vs. struggling vs. toxic mentoring relationship), so when rooms reconvene, groups report out and the class can compare across the spectrum. The instructor closes by asking students to privately drop one word in the chat that describes their current mentoring reality — present, absent, or longed for — generating a live word cloud that anchors the discussion personally.
activities.materials: 3-4 pre-written mentor scenario documents; shared rubric doc with Fred Smith's eight characteristics listed; breakout room functionality enabled
activities.learningGoal: Applies Fred Smith's eight characteristics diagnostically to real relationship dynamics and builds evaluative language for mentor-mentee relationships
activities.scalingNotes: For 25 students, 5 groups of 5 works well with 3-4 unique scenarios (some groups share a scenario). For larger cohorts, add scenarios. For smaller cohorts under 15, run as a full-group fishbowl with one scenario.

activities.title: The Two-Column Reveal: Journaling in Real Time
activities.description: The instructor briefly models Douglas Rumford's two-column reflective loop method on screen — showing a live example with Column A as the raw experience/observation and Column B as the meaning or leadership implication drawn from it. Students then open a private journal doc or the platform's journaling feature and spend 5 minutes writing a genuine two-column entry about a leadership moment from the past two weeks, using the structured format. After writing, the instructor invites 3-4 volunteers to share only their Column B (the insight/implication) — not the raw event — protecting privacy while making meaning visible to the group. The debrief asks: 'What disciplines did you feel resistance toward as you wrote?' This surfaces Rumford's journaling disciplines as lived experience rather than lecture content.
activities.materials: Digital journal space (platform journal feature, Google Doc, or personal notes app); instructor demo slide showing a completed two-column example
activities.learningGoal: Practices the two-column reflective loop method and identifies personal friction points within Rumford's journaling disciplines
activities.scalingNotes: This activity works identically across group sizes since writing is individual. For larger groups, use a poll or reaction emoji instead of open sharing to surface common Column B themes quickly.

activities.title: Core Values Mentor Speed Draft: Criteria to Candidate
activities.description: Students are given a 2-minute timed prompt to write, without editing, the name of one person who immediately comes to mind when they think of someone who lives their values with integrity — not their closest friend, not a celebrity, but someone they actually have access to. The instructor then walks the group through the core criteria for a Core Values mentor, displayed on screen, and students spend 4 minutes privately scoring their candidate against each criterion using a simple 1-3 scale in a shared form or their journal. Pairs are formed via breakout rooms for 5 minutes to pressure-test each other's choice: 'Tell me one criterion your candidate clearly meets and one you're uncertain about.' The activity closes with a full-group commitment prompt in the chat: students type the first initial of their candidate's name and one concrete next step (e.g., 'J — send email by Friday'), making the intention social and specific without sacrificing privacy.
activities.materials: Criteria checklist (pre-built as a shared form or PDF); breakout room functionality; chat enabled for commitment closing
activities.learningGoal: Moves students from abstract mentor criteria to a concrete candidate and a committed first contact action step
activities.scalingNotes: Pairs work well for 25. For cohorts over 30, use groups of 3 for the pressure-test conversation. For async versions, the criteria self-scoring and commitment step can be submitted as a discussion board post.

activities.title: Roots Under Pressure: Purpose Stress-Test Debate
activities.description: The instructor poses a provocative prompt on screen: 'A leader without a clear purpose is just a manager with a title — agree or disagree?' Students vote silently using a poll, then the room is split into two groups based on their vote — Agree and Disagree — and placed into breakout rooms for 6 minutes to build their strongest collective argument, drawing specifically on the Roots (purpose) layer of the Leadership Tree framework. When the full group reconvenes, each side selects one spokesperson to make a 90-second case, after which the instructor opens 2 minutes of cross-debate via unmuted open floor or chat. The session closes with a flip: the instructor asks members of each side to name one thing the opposing argument got right, practicing the intellectual humility that effective leaders need alongside strong purpose. This reframes purpose not as a poster on the wall, but as a living, contested, necessary root.
activities.materials: Poll tool (platform-native or Mentimeter/Poll Everywhere); no additional materials needed
activities.learningGoal: Deepens understanding of purpose as the foundational Roots layer and practices articulating and defending leadership convictions under social pressure
activities.scalingNotes: For groups under 15, skip breakout rooms and run the debate as a full-group live discussion with the instructor facilitating sides. For groups over 35, add a third 'undecided' group tasked with identifying the strongest argument from each side.

activities.title: The Trunk Audit: Mapping and Gaps in Your Support Network
activities.description: Students open a personal mapping template (provided as a simple shared slide they duplicate, or drawn freehand in their journal) showing three concentric rings: innermost ring is 'actively investing in me,' middle ring is 'available but underutilized,' outer ring is 'potential I haven't tapped.' They spend 5 minutes placing real people from their lives into the rings, then answer two reflection questions embedded in the template: 'Where is my trunk thinnest?' and 'Which branch or leaf of my leadership (DISC/LSA) is least supported by my current network?' Students drop their answers to the second question in the chat simultaneously on a countdown, creating a visible map of collective gaps across the cohort. The instructor uses the chat responses to identify 2-3 patterns and name them aloud — this models the kind of systemic pattern-recognition that leaders with strong trunks develop over time.
activities.materials: Concentric ring template (pre-built in slides or Miro); chat enabled for simultaneous drop
activities.learningGoal: Applies the Trunk metaphor diagnostically to reveal real gaps in the student's current support network and connects network gaps to specific leadership style needs
activities.scalingNotes: Works well at any size since the mapping is individual and the share is via chat. For synchronous cohorts over 40, use a word cloud tool instead of open chat to surface patterns more cleanly.

slides.title: The Leadership Tree: Roots, Trunk, Branches, and the Harvest of Your Design
slides.bodyText: What if everything you've been given — your purpose, your people, your personality — was never meant to be understood in pieces?
slides.speakerNotes: Welcome students to Session 4. Tell them this is the integrative session — the one where the assessments, the relationships, and the inner work stop living in separate buckets and start forming a whole portrait. Set the expectation: by the end of today, they should be able to describe their leadership identity using one coherent metaphor — the tree. Ask: 'When you look at a tree, what part do you notice first?' Then tell them: most leaders only ever manage the branches.

slides.title: The Tree as Mirror
slides.bodyText: Before we talk about what you do as a leader, we have to understand what holds you up — and what holds you together.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition into the first major section. Ask students to set aside everything they think they know about leadership style and effectiveness for a moment. The tree metaphor works because it forces us to ask: 'What's underground?' Most leadership development starts with the branches — skills, behaviors, outputs — but this course starts with the roots. Orient them: we'll move from roots up to the harvest.

slides.title: Roots and Trunk: Your Foundation
bullets: Roots = Purpose — the 'why' beneath all you do
Roots draw nutrients from calling, values, and conviction
Trunk = Support Network — those who hold you upright
Trunk includes mentors, peers, and accountability partners
No trunk = branches that snap under pressure
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each element slowly. For roots, ask: 'If someone cut your leadership down to the ground tomorrow, what would grow back?' That's your root system — your purpose. For the trunk, use a vivid image: a palm tree survives hurricanes because its trunk bends without breaking. A leader's support network provides that same flexibility under load. Ask students: 'Who is your trunk right now? Can you name them?' Let a few share. This often surfaces how isolated many emerging leaders feel.

slides.title: Branches & Leaves: DISC + LSA as One Portrait
bullets: DISC: How you behave under pressure and in relationship
Natural style vs. Adapted style
Observable patterns others already see in you
LSA: Why you lead — values, calling, spiritual motivation
slides.bodyText: ["LSA surfaces the interior engine, not just the exterior gears", "DISC shows the what; LSA illuminates the why", "Together: a 360° leadership identity", "Neither is complete without the other"]
slides.speakerNotes: This is the key integration moment of the course. Remind students they've now completed both assessments. The mistake most people make is treating them as separate data points — like two unrelated blood tests. Instead, model for them how to hold both at once: 'I'm a High-D on DISC, which means I drive toward results fast. My LSA reveals that my deepest motivation is justice. So I push hard — but I push hardest when something unfair is happening.' Ask 2-3 students to try their own integration out loud. Celebrate complexity.

slides.title: The Key Insight
slides.bodyText: You are not a collection of assessment scores. You are an ecosystem — and a healthy ecosystem requires roots, structure, and growth working together.
slides.speakerNotes: Pause here. Read it once, then let silence sit for 5 seconds. Ask: 'What part of your ecosystem has been most neglected — roots, trunk, or branches?' This is the kind of question worth journaling on. Many students will realize they've been frantically growing branches (skills, credentials, projects) while the roots are shallow and the trunk is thin. Don't rush past this slide.

slides.title: Mentoring That Matters
slides.bodyText: A support network isn't accidental — it's architected. This section is about who belongs in your trunk and how to build those relationships with intention.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition by acknowledging that 'networking' is a word that makes many people cringe — it feels transactional. Reframe: this section is about the difference between a contact and a mentor. Fred Smith, a businessman and lifelong mentor to leaders including Howard Hendricks, gave us a framework for what mentoring relationships that actually transform people look like. Tell students: Smith mentored across decades and his wisdom here is hard-earned, not theoretical.

slides.title: Fred Smith, Author & Mentor to Thousands
slides.bodyText: "A good mentor doesn't just answer your questions — he questions your answers."
slides.speakerNotes: Let the quote breathe. Ask: 'Who has done this for you — not given you answers, but pushed back on the answers you've already settled on?' This is the rarer, more valuable form of mentoring. Smith believed that the most dangerous moment in a leader's development is when they stop being questioned. This quote sets up the eight characteristics because it reframes what mentoring actually is: not advice-dispensing, but truth-telling within a trusted relationship.

slides.title: Smith's Eight Marks of Effective Mentoring
bullets: Mutual respect — both parties bring something real to the table
Honest, candid communication — safety to say hard things
Defined purpose — not just coffee, but a shared agenda
Regular, consistent contact — momentum requires rhythm
Accountability — commitments made are commitments tracked
Confidentiality — what's shared stays shared only with trust
Natural relational chemistry — forced mentoring rarely works
Developmental trajectory — the mentee is visibly growing
slides.speakerNotes: Don't just list these — pause on #2 and #7. On honesty: ask students 'Have you ever had a mentor who only encouraged you? What did that cost you?' On chemistry: validate that it's okay if a mentoring relationship doesn't click. Forced mentoring is worse than no mentoring. On #8, the developmental trajectory — this is the test: after six months, can both parties point to specific growth? If not, something in the structure needs to change. Invite students to evaluate any current mentoring relationships against this list.

slides.title: Finding Your Core Values Mentor
bullets: Embodies values you aspire to — not just someone successful
Demonstrates integrity across public and private life
Willing to speak honestly, even uncomfortably
Has capacity and genuine willingness to invest
slides.bodyText: ["Identify 2–3 candidates this week", "Pray, reflect, and discern before approaching", "Extend a specific, humble invitation", "Propose a 6–12 month commitment with clear rhythm", "Agree on frequency, format, and focus areas"]
slides.speakerNotes: Left column = who to look for. Right column = how to move. Stress that the invitation matters — most people never get a mentor because they never ask. Coach students on how to ask: be specific ('I'd like to meet with you monthly for 6 months around the theme of X'), be humble ('I'm not looking for answers, I'm looking for someone to think alongside me'), and be grateful regardless of the answer. Role-play the ask briefly if time allows. Set a concrete deadline: names on paper by next class.

slides.title: The Reflective Loop
slides.bodyText: Information without reflection produces activity. Reflection without discipline produces sentimentality. What we need is a structured practice that turns experience into wisdom.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition to the third major section. Ask: 'How many of you journal?' Note the hands. Then ask: 'How many of you journal in a way that actually changes how you lead?' Fewer hands. Douglas Rumford, a pastor and spiritual formation scholar, identified that most journaling fails not because people lack insight, but because they lack a repeatable method. His two-column reflective loop gives structure to an otherwise shapeless practice. Preview: this isn't about filling pages — it's about creating distance between your experience and your reaction so wisdom can enter.

slides.title: Douglas Rumford, Spiritual Formation Scholar
slides.bodyText: "The journal is not a diary of what happened — it is a laboratory where you discover what it meant."
slides.speakerNotes: Ask students: what's the difference between a diary and a laboratory? A diary records. A laboratory tests hypotheses. Rumford's insight is that most people journal as reporters — 'Here's what happened today' — and never become scientists — 'Here's what I'm learning about how I respond, what I fear, where I grow.' This reframe is the entire point of the two-column method. Let that distinction sink in before moving to the mechanics.

slides.title: The Two-Column Reflective Loop
bullets: Left column: Observation — what happened, what you noticed
Right column: Reflection — what it means, what it reveals
No editing in the moment — write first, analyze second
Weekly review discipline: look for patterns across entries
Monthly integration: what is God or growth saying consistently?
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through the mechanics slowly. Left column = reporter mode: describe the experience as it was, without judgment. Right column = interpreter mode: what does this reveal about your values, your fears, your leadership defaults? The key discipline is separation — you write the left column completely before you ever touch the right. This forces you to observe before you conclude. For the weekly review, have students ask: 'Where did I see my roots show up? Where was my trunk missing?' For the monthly integration, it's a prayerful, slow read-back — not analysis but listening. Recommend 10–15 minutes daily, not an hour.

slides.title: The Harvest
slides.bodyText: The harvest of your design is not what you produce — it is who you become, and what grows in others because of your presence.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the culminating big idea of the entire chapter. Read it slowly. Then ask: 'What does a tree actually give away?' Shade, fruit, oxygen, shelter — none of which the tree experiences itself. The leader who tends their roots, invests in their trunk, understands their branches, and practices reflection — that leader eventually produces a harvest they don't fully see. This is the long game. Ask students: 'Who do you want to have been shaped by your leadership ten years from now?' Let that question sit. It's worth writing about.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: The Leadership Tree integrates purpose, network, and personality into one portrait
DISC + LSA together reveal both how and why you lead
Effective mentoring requires eight conditions — chemistry and honesty above all
Your Core Values Mentor: identify, discern, and invite — this week
Rumford's two-column loop turns daily experience into cumulative wisdom
slides.speakerNotes: Close the loop on the whole session. Give students 90 seconds to write their single most important takeaway in their own words — not from this list, but in their own voice. This is the first left-column entry for their two-column journal this week. Remind them of the two assignments moving forward: (1) name their Core Values Mentor candidate by next session, and (2) begin the two-column journal today — not tomorrow. End with this: 'A tree doesn't decide to grow. It grows because the conditions are right. Your job is to tend the conditions.'

blocks.title: Audiobook
blocks.transcript:
Consider Marcus, a youth director who has been running three weekly programs, a mentoring cohort, a summer camp, and a community mural project simultaneously for six years. When people in the city think of urban youth ministry, they think of Marcus. His calendar is full. His social media presence is full. His building is full. But Marcus hasn't revisited his personal purpose statement since he wrote it in a seminary class nearly a decade ago. He hasn't met regularly with a mentor since the one person who filled that role moved to another state two years ago. And when his D-I-S-C report arrived last week, he found himself staring at the words "Adapted Style" for a long time, realizing he has been performing a leadership role that was designed to impress others rather than express who he actually is. Marcus's branches are spectacular. His roots have been quietly dying. [short pause]

Sessions one through three of this workshop have equipped you with three distinct and powerful lenses for self-understanding: a personal purpose statement rooted in the three-fold mission, a D-I-S-C Natural Style profile revealing your behavioral wiring, and an L-S-A — Leadership Style Assessment — strategy report mapping how you actually deploy leadership toward those you serve. This session is the first major synthesis point of the program — the moment when those three streams of data converge into a single, integrated portrait called the LEADERSHIP TREE. This session is not an ending; it is an orientation. The tree you build today will be the map you carry into every subsequent session, every group dialogue, and ultimately into your Breakthrough Plan. [short pause]

The Leadership Tree is the D-V-U-L-I program's signature synthesis tool. Its power lies not in its complexity but in its completeness. Unlike analytical frameworks that isolate a single dimension of leadership — personality alone, or strategy alone, or purpose alone — the tree metaphor insists that all three must coexist and work together if a leader is to thrive. As Gosling and Mintzberg argued in 2003, effective leadership development requires integrative metaphors: models that hold multiple dimensions simultaneously, allowing learners to perceive relationships and tensions rather than isolated competencies. The tree is precisely such a model. [short pause]

The DVULI workbook frames the three structural elements this way. The leaves and branches are the parts of your leadership that are visible to others — your behavioral style and your leadership strategy. The trunk represents the people who support you, just as the trunk of a living tree supports its branches. And the roots, though invisible to onlookers, are just as extensive and important as everything above ground. They include a clearly defined purpose. [short pause]

What makes this architecture theologically as well as pedagogically compelling is the insistence that a healthy tree can live for a very long time, but one with damaged or underdeveloped parts soon dies. This is not primarily a metaphor for ministry programs. It is a metaphor for the leader themselves. Research by Harbaugh and Furst in 1994 found that urban ministry leaders face significantly elevated burnout risk compared to other pastoral contexts, and that the three primary predictors are role ambiguity — that is, unclear purpose — social isolation, meaning an inadequate support network — and unprocessed stress, which results from the absence of reflective practices. That finding maps with striking precision onto the three structural elements of the Leadership Tree. When Marcus's roots begin to die, it is only a matter of time before the entire tree follows, no matter how impressive the canopy appears to onlookers. [short pause]

The ROOTS: Purpose as the Invisible Foundation. [short pause]

In a living tree, roots are the last part of the structure anyone sees. They extend deep into the ground, far wider than the visible canopy above, working in silence to draw up everything the tree needs to survive and bear fruit. Remove the roots and the tree — no matter how magnificent it appears — will be dead within a season. The same is true of leadership. As Craig and Snook found in 2014, in their research with Harvard Business School executives, fewer than twenty percent of leaders carry a clear, actionable personal purpose, and that gap between purpose and action is the primary factor in leadership derailment. The leader who has not settled the question of "why am I here?" will eventually find themselves unable to answer "where am I going?" with any conviction. [short pause]

In the first session, you worked through the three-fold mission from Richard Bolles's book What Color Is Your Parachute? and drafted a personal purpose statement. Recall the three layers. Purpose Part One: to know God and enjoy Him forever. Purpose Part Two: to make the world a better place moment by moment. And Purpose Part Three: to exercise the particular talent you came to earth to use, in the setting God has most prepared you for, toward the purposes He most needs done. Your purpose statement was the attempt to synthesize these three layers into a sentence or two you could carry with you. That statement is what belongs in the roots of your Leadership Tree. [short pause]

There is an important developmental note in the DVULI workbook's treatment of purpose: "Our purpose doesn't change; our understanding and growth in it may. As our roles or settings change, the way we express our purpose may stay the same, shift, or expand." This means that the roots grow deeper over the course of the program. The purpose statement you bring to this session is a beginning, not a final answer. In the Second National Conference, you will revisit your personal purpose statement in the context of systems thinking. At the conclusion of the program, your Breakthrough Plan will be anchored in the purpose you have been refining all along. As Bill George and colleagues found in 2007, in their landmark study of one hundred and twenty-five diverse leaders, authentic leadership emerges precisely from this kind of ongoing, story-rooted engagement with one's own purpose — not from adopting external frameworks, but from the patient clarification of what one's own life has been saying all along. [short pause]

Before reading on, take a moment to locate the personal purpose statement you wrote in the first session. Read it aloud if you can. Does it still feel accurate? Does it feel alive? Does it feel like something you would actually want to build your leadership on — or does it feel more like an academic exercise you completed to move on to the next task? The honesty with which you answer that question is the beginning of healthy roots. [short pause]

The TRUNK: Support as the Hidden Structure. [short pause]

A tree's trunk is its most visible structural feature, and yet most people see it merely as the conduit between roots and branches — not the focus itself. In the Leadership Tree, however, the trunk carries specific meaning: it represents the people who support and pray for the leader. Two categories matter here — people who pray for the leader regularly, and people who support and encourage them. This is not incidental. The trunk, in the life of a real tree, is the load-bearing column that allows a tree to stand upright against wind and weather and to channel nutrients from the roots to the canopy. A tree with a compromised trunk will eventually collapse under the weight of its own branches, regardless of how healthy the roots and leaves may be. [short pause]

Kram's foundational research on mentoring in 1985 introduced a concept that directly illuminates the trunk: the MENTOR CONSTELLATION. Rather than positing a single transformative mentor relationship, Kram found that effective development occurs when leaders maintain a network of diverse support relationships, each serving different functions. Some relationships primarily serve career-development functions — sponsorship, exposure, coaching, protection, and challenging assignments. Others serve psychosocial functions — role modeling, acceptance, counseling, and friendship. Both categories are essential; neither is sufficient alone. The people who belong in the trunk of your Leadership Tree are not only formal mentors. They are everyone who holds your development in their prayers and provides the relational scaffolding that lets you grow. [short pause]

As Daloz argued in 1999, effective developmental relationships involve three core functions: support, which provides the emotional safety in which growth becomes possible; challenge, which disrupts the comfortable but limiting assumptions that keep a leader from realizing their potential; and vision, which means holding a clear picture of who the mentee can become before the mentee can see it for themselves. Notice that support alone — without challenge and vision — can create comfort without growth. Trunk-sized relationships, in Daloz's terms, are not simply people who make you feel good about yourself. They are people willing to tell you the truth. [short pause]

The DVULI workbook is deliberately open-ended about the trunk: the health of your support network is entirely your own work. No assessment tool can tell you whether you have a thriving community of people who pray for you and speak truth into your life. Only you know whether the names you would write in the trunk represent real, active relationships — or whether that section of your tree is, in Marcus's words, already hollow. [short pause]

The BRANCHES AND LEAVES: The Visible Expression of Your Design. [short pause]

While roots and trunk do their quiet, foundational work, branches and leaves are what the world sees. They are the observable, outward expression of the leader's design — the behavioral patterns and strategic preferences that others experience when they encounter your leadership in action. Together, the elements in this section answer the question that every person you lead is unconsciously asking: How does this leader tend to show up? [short pause]

The branches section draws from two sources. The first is your D-I-S-C Natural Style — your style pattern name, your style pattern letters, your highest plot point letter and its associated traits, and the Biblical character you have connected to your natural style. The second is your L-S-A strategy profile — your most frequently chosen leadership strategy and your least frequently chosen one. [short pause]

A leader with a high Dominance natural style will show up with directness and a push-for-results energy. A leader with a high Steadiness orientation will show up with patient, open supportiveness. Neither is superior; both reflect the diverse design of the body of Christ. First Peter, chapter four, verses ten and eleven captures this beautifully in its opening devotional premise within the DVULI program: every believer has been entrusted with gifts as stewardship, not ownership. Our behavioral style is not something we chose; it is something we have been given to manage wisely for the sake of others. [short pause]

The L-S-A dimension of the branches adds a critical layer that D-I-S-C alone cannot provide. While D-I-S-C reveals how you tend to behave, the L-S-A reveals how you tend to deploy your leadership energy in relation to specific task-and-performer combinations. Your most frequently chosen strategy indicates the default mode to which you naturally return under pressure or when you are not paying close attention. Your least frequently chosen strategy points to the developmental frontier — the mode that may be most needed by members of your team but least available in your natural repertoire. As Kolb showed in 1984, learning deepens only when all four stages of the experiential cycle are engaged. Similarly, leadership effectiveness deepens only when all four situational strategies are accessible and flexibly deployed. The branches of your tree map both your current strengths and your growing edges simultaneously. [short pause]

Mentoring That Matters: Fred Smith's Eight Characteristics. [short pause]

The second half of this session shifts from mapping your current state to building the disciplines that will sustain growth throughout the entire arc of the program and beyond. The first of these disciplines is mentoring. The DVULI program structures a very specific mentoring relationship — one with a Core Values mentor whose primary role is helping you understand and apply the program's Core Values to your life and ministry. Fred Smith's article "Mentoring That Matters" provides the human and relational architecture within which that formal relationship must operate. [short pause]

Smith opens with a crucial distinction: genuine mentoring is, in his words, "a one-on-one relationship between a mentor and mentee for the specific and definable development of a skill or an art." Church programs that pair older men with younger men for general Bible study or prayer are not, in Smith's view, mentoring — they are fellowship, which is valuable but different. Mentoring requires a specific agenda, a directional purpose, and a willingness on both sides to inhabit an asymmetrical learning relationship rather than a peer one. This precision of definition matters enormously in the DVULI context, because the Core Values mentor is not simply a friend or an accountability partner. The mentor is being asked to hold the mentee accountable to growth in specific, articulated dimensions of leadership character. [short pause]

Smith identifies eight characteristics that must be present in some combination for mentoring to be effective. It is worth sitting with each one carefully, because they reveal as much about the ideal mentee posture as they do about the ideal mentor. [short pause]

The first is compatible philosophy. Mentor and mentee must share foundational commitments. For DVULI purposes, this means a shared commitment to Christ and to the Core Values the program is built upon. Philosophy shapes method; misaligned philosophy produces frustration rather than growth. [short pause]

The second is subject knowledge with objective criticism. A mentor who simply says what the mentee wants to hear is, in Smith's striking phrase, irresponsible. The mentor must be knowledgeable enough to evaluate accurately and honest enough to say so — including saying "I don't know" when that is the truth. [short pause]

The third is genuine belief in the mentee's potential. Without this conviction, the mentor will not invest the cognitive and emotional energy the relationship demands. Crucially, this belief must survive the mentee's own failure of confidence — which will happen. [short pause]

The fourth is the ability to help define vision, goal, and plan. Smith's metaphor is vivid here. He compares it to choosing a spouse: you could have married several different people, but at some point you chose one. The mentor gives the gift of focus by helping the mentee choose one direction among the many possibilities that capable leaders always face. [short pause]

The fifth is good chemistry expressed through clear communication. Smith's test is direct: before beginning work with someone, he checks this by talking for a few minutes and then asking the person to repeat what he has said. If communication is routinely misread in both directions, the relationship will produce confusion rather than clarity. [short pause]

The sixth is the experience to develop options rather than decisions. A good mentor does not take over the decision-making process. The mentor expands the field of options from which the mentee makes their own informed choices. As Smith puts it, "A good mentor is not a quick-fix artist." [short pause]

The seventh is commitment to see the process through. Smith's image — "I don't want to get you halfway across the river" — captures a fundamental ethical obligation of the mentor relationship. Abandoning a mentee midway through a developmental season is a form of harm. [short pause]

The eighth is permission to hold the mentee accountable. The mentee must explicitly grant this permission; accountability cannot be imposed. Smith's analogy is memorable: "Accountability is like a tail on the kite — it keeps things from darting around." [short pause]

Smith also outlines the responsibilities of the mentee, which are sometimes overlooked in the excitement of finding a good mentor. The effective mentee admits ignorance freely. They work to ask the right questions by thinking carefully before speaking. They do their homework before every meeting. They never attempt to use the mentor for social capital or name-dropping. And they grow visibly over time — because, as Smith says, "progress is the pay the student gives the mentor." This is a demanding posture. It requires the kind of intellectual and spiritual humility that is genuinely countercultural in achievement-oriented ministry environments. [short pause]

Before you begin the mentor candidate process, reflect honestly on this: What has been your track record as a mentee? Have you brought prepared questions and done your homework? Have you made your growth visible to those who have invested in you? Or have previous mentoring relationships faded because you expected the mentor to drive the agenda? Smith's framework places the primary responsibility for the relationship's vitality on the mentee, not the mentor. Sit with that for a moment. [short pause]

The DVULI Core Values Mentor: Criteria and Process. [short pause]

The DVULI workbook specifies a set of non-negotiable criteria that a Core Values mentor must meet. These criteria are designed to ensure that the relationship actually serves the program's developmental purposes rather than simply providing pleasant conversation. There are five core qualities. First, the mentor must be a mature, committed Christian who will give honest feedback. Second, they must be someone who demonstrably applies the Core Values in their personal and professional life. Third, they must have personally executed a strategic, significant, and sustained personal or career breakthrough — a breakthrough in the DVULI-defined sense. Fourth, they must demonstrate proven leadership qualities. And fifth, they must be willing to attend a two-hour orientation and meet monthly in person for one year and possibly longer. [short pause]

Additional structural requirements deserve emphasis. The mentor must be of the same gender. The mentor must not be a current supervisor, pastor, or family member. The mentor must be willing and able to commit to monthly face-to-face meetings — not phone calls or video conference as a substitute. And crucially, the mentor's most important gift to the mentee is their time. A candidate who leads a very busy life and struggles to return messages or maintain commitments is not well-suited to this role, regardless of how impressive their credentials may appear. [short pause]

As Daloz noted in 1999, effective mentors do not need to be perfect models — they need to be honest companions. The most dangerous false criterion in mentor selection is prestige. A nationally recognized leader with limited time and emotional bandwidth will serve a mentee less well than a committed local leader who genuinely believes in the mentee's potential and will actually show up. [short pause]

The DVULI timeline for mentor engagement is specific. Identify candidates before the end of this workshop. Consult with your City Coordinator. Invite your chosen candidate by providing them the "What Is a DVULI Mentor?" handout. Secure their commitment prior to the First National Conference and begin monthly meetings in May and June. The agenda for every meeting is the mentee's responsibility, not the mentor's. Begin with your goals related to the Core Values, return to those goals at each meeting, and be willing to be held accountable for what you commit to between sessions. [short pause]

Reflective Journaling: The Discipline That Deepens Everything Else. [short pause]

The second sustaining discipline introduced in this session is REFLECTIVE JOURNALING. Douglas Rumford's article "The Value of a Personal Journal" offers both the theological grounding and the practical framework for the kind of journaling DVULI asks participants to maintain. Rumford's opening image is striking: "How is it that when a person prays for illumination and receives it, he or she can let that precious truth slip away like the tide erasing writing in the sand?" The journal exists to catch what would otherwise be lost. [short pause]

In his landmark 1983 work on professional practice, Donald Schön provides the theoretical scaffolding for understanding what Rumford was intuiting pastorally. Schön distinguished between reflection-in-action — the kind of thinking we do while doing, adjusting in real time — and REFLECTION-ON-ACTION, the retrospective sense-making we do after an experience has concluded. Expert practitioners in complex professions — and urban ministry is precisely such a profession — develop their competence not through the linear application of learned rules, but through iterative cycles of returning to experience with new data, revising their understanding each time they do so. Schön called this spiral movement the REFLECTIVE LOOP: the practitioner does not simply move forward chronologically but returns, again and again, to earlier frames of understanding and finds them transformed by what has happened since. [short pause]

Rumford names this same dynamic with his own term: spiraling. "This is my own term," he writes, "for going over the same ground again and again. The center of the spiral, the issue, may be the same, but our understanding of it is continually deepening and progressing like the widening loops of a spiral." The Personal Learning Journal you will maintain throughout DVULI and complete as part of your Breakthrough Plan is not a diary of events. It is not an academic paper. It is not a record of other people's ideas. The DVULI workbook is explicit: in your journal, you are the only expert that matters. It is a structured space for reflecting intelligently on what and how you are learning, including how you are applying those lessons and what outcomes you are achieving as a result. [short pause]

The DVULI two-column format for journaling is intentionally counterintuitive. The right column — labeled "Response to Events" — is written first and contemporaneously, capturing immediate reactions to learning experiences while they are still fresh. The left column — labeled "Reflective Loop: Deeper Insights" — is written later, after time has passed and new experiences have added data. This temporal gap is itself a pedagogical tool. As Kolb showed in 1984, the movement from concrete experience to reflective observation to abstract conceptualization requires time and distance. Compressing that movement eliminates the insight the cycle is designed to generate. [short pause]

Rumford's practical principles for journaling are worth holding alongside Schön's theoretical framework. Trust the Holy Spirit to guide each session. Work with feelings and perceptions rather than a chronicle of dates and events. Trust your own insights. Be completely free and write for your eyes only. Be honest — "don't fool yourself with pious talk," Rumford says. Discipline yourself to eventually seek the promise, not just state the facts. And practice spiraling — returning to earlier entries with the eyes of new experience and finding them charged with meaning you could not have seen when you first wrote them. [short pause]

To put this into practice now, consider reflecting on one of the following questions in writing. What surprised you about your D-I-S-C Natural Style, and what confirmed what you already sensed about yourself? Where do you see your most and least frequently chosen L-S-A strategies playing out with the people you lead? What feels alive in your personal purpose statement, and what feels incomplete? What is thriving in your Leadership Tree as a whole, and what is struggling? Or how did you respond to Fred Smith's eight characteristics of effective mentoring? Write your immediate response now. Then return to it in a few days — and notice what the left column has to say. [short pause]

The Harvest of Integration: Carrying Your Tree Forward. [short pause]

The Leadership Tree you complete in this session is not an exercise you will file away. It is a living document — a reference point to which you will return in every subsequent session, every group dialogue, and every mentoring conversation. When you encounter the Core Values of Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage in the First National Conference, you will be evaluating them in relation to a specific leader — yourself — whose roots, trunk, and branches you have now named with precision. When you engage systems thinking in the Second National Conference, you will be examining the systems that either nourish or threaten the health of your specific tree. When you write your Breakthrough Plan, the roots of your tree will be the foundation from which your Personal Focus Statement grows. [short pause]

As George and colleagues found in 2007, leaders with clear personal purpose and strong support networks — what they called "true north groups" — consistently outperform and outlast leaders without them. They also identified what they called "crucible moments" — formative experiences that, when reflected upon honestly, reveal the core values and leadership purpose that have always been present beneath the surface. Your Leadership Tree is, in essence, a crucible document. It catches the insights that emerged from your own crucible moments and gives them a structure through which they can be shared, tested, and built upon. [short pause]

Both mentoring and journaling are not supplemental activities. They are the primary disciplines through which DVULI learning is converted into breakthrough — the roots through which the whole program draws its deepest nourishment. Every time you return to an earlier journal entry and discover a deeper layer in the left column, you are practicing the reflective loop that Schön identified as the hallmark of expert practitioners. Every time you show up to your monthly mentoring meeting with prepared questions and evidence of growth, you are modeling for the emerging leaders in your ministry what it looks like to be a learner rather than a knower. [short pause]

The claim at the heart of this session is simple but demanding: a healthy tree produces fruit over a very long time, but only if all three structural systems are intact and actively tended. Roots without trunk collapse under the weight of isolation. Trunk without roots becomes a hollow shell — supportive in appearance but nourishing nothing. Branches without roots and trunk are simply cut flowers: beautiful for a season, then dry. This program is making a long bet on your willingness to tend your whole tree — not just the parts of it that other people can see. [short pause]

To bring together what this session has covered: the Leadership Tree integrates your personal purpose as roots, your support network as trunk, and your D-I-S-C Natural Style plus L-S-A strategies as branches and leaves — all forming a single, portable portrait of your divine design. Roots supply the invisible nourishment from which all visible growth flows. A leader without a clear, living purpose will eventually drift no matter how impressive their programs appear. The trunk, held up by the people who support and pray for you, is the load-bearing structure that allows the leader to stand under pressure and grow sustainably — and its health is entirely the leader's own responsibility to cultivate. The branches and leaves reveal how you are currently showing up for those you lead: both your strengths and your growing edges, in the service of greater effectiveness and flexibility. [short pause]

Effective mentoring, in Fred Smith's vision, requires compatible philosophy, honest feedback, genuine belief in the mentee's potential, skill at defining vision and plan, clear chemistry, options rather than imposed decisions, sustained commitment, and explicitly granted accountability. The Core Values mentor must demonstrate a personal breakthrough in the DVULI sense, be of the same gender, be willing to meet monthly in person, and must not be a supervisor, family member, or current pastor. And reflective journaling, as both Rumford and Schön illuminate it, operates through the spiral: immediate response in the right column, deepened insight in the left column, returned to again and again as new experience provides fresh lenses. The temporal gap between writing in the right column and returning to write in the left is not incidental — it is essential to the learning the reflective loop is designed to generate. [short pause]

Looking ahead: this session completes the first arc of the DVULI program — the arc of self-knowledge. Beginning with the First National Conference and the introduction of the five Core Values of Accountability, Balance, Interdependence, Empowerment, and Leverage, the program pivots from inward reflection to outward application. You will bring your Leadership Tree — now complete — into a world of systemic challenges, community assets, and strategic futures. The question shifts from "Who am I?" to "What will I do with who I am?" Come to the First National Conference having met with at least one potential mentor candidate and having completed at least two right-column journal entries in your two-column journal. The left column will be waiting.

blocks.title: Infographic
blocks.prompt: Create a detailed educational infographic in 16:9 landscape format with a light cream background (#faf8f5). The centerpiece is a large, elegant, stylized tree diagram occupying the middle 40% of the canvas, drawn with clean vector-style lines and subtle gradients — not cartoonish, but refined and architectural. The tree has four clearly labeled zones from bottom to top: ROOTS below the soil line, TRUNK rising from the ground, BRANCHES and LEAVES forming the canopy, and FRUIT/HARVEST at the outermost edges of the canopy. A thin horizontal ground line separates the root zone from the trunk. The ROOTS section uses deep #00558C blue with three thick root tendrils labeled "Purpose," "Core Values," and "Identity." The TRUNK section is rendered in warm brown-gold (#d97706) and labeled "Support Network" with small icon badges along the trunk showing "Mentors," "Peers," and "Accountability Partners." The BRANCHES section in #319B41 green splits into two main limbs: one labeled "DISC Profile" with four smaller branch tips labeled D, I, S, C, and another labeled "LSA Strengths" with smaller tips. The canopy area is labeled "Integrated Leadership Portrait." Small fruit icons at the branch tips are labeled "Harvest: Influence & Impact." On the LEFT side of the tree, place a vertical panel titled "Mentoring That Matters" in #00558C with eight short horizontal bars listing Fred Smith's eight characteristics of effective mentor-mentee relationships: "1. Mutual Respect," "2. Shared Values," "3. Honest Dialogue," "4. Commitment to Growth," "5. Confidentiality," "6. Availability," "7. Accountability," "8. Appropriate Season." Each bar has a small circular checkbox icon. On the RIGHT side of the tree, place a vertical panel titled "Reflective Journaling" in #319B41 showing the two-column reflective loop method as a simple diagram: two columns side by side labeled "Experience / Event" and "Reflection / Insight" with curved arrows looping between them forming a cycle, and three discipline labels beneath: "Consistency," "Honesty," "Review." Below this right panel, add a smaller box titled "Core Values Mentor Action Plan" in #d97706 with three sequential steps connected by arrows: "Define Criteria → Identify Candidates → Set Timeline." At the very top center, place the header text "THE LEADERSHIP TREE" in large bold serif font colored #00558C, with a subtitle beneath in smaller italic text: "Roots, Trunk, Branches & the Harvest of Your Design." Use clean sans-serif fonts for all labels. Maintain generous white space between elements. Use subtle drop shadows on the two side panels. Add thin dotted connector lines from the left panel to the trunk area and from the right panel to the roots area to show how mentoring strengthens support and journaling deepens purpose. The overall aesthetic should be polished, modern, and professional — suitable for a graduate-level leadership development course.

config.concept: The Leadership Tree as integrated leadership portrait
config.description: A visual, SVG-based Leadership Tree where participants click each section (roots, trunk, branches) to enter or display their assessment data. The Roots section prompts for the three-fold purpose statement from Session 1 and shows it as the nourishing foundation. The Trunk section contains a scrollable grid where participants rate three mentor/support candidates on five criteria (knowledge of Core Values, track record of breakthrough, demonstrated leadership, willingness to meet monthly, gender match) using a 1–4 scale. The Branches section auto-populates with their DISC Natural Style pattern name, Natural Style letters, and Chosen Biblical Character from Session 2, plus their most and least-frequently chosen LSA strategies from Session 3. When complete, the full tree renders as a shareable image.
config.rationale: Building the Leadership Tree interactively — rather than filling in a paper poster — allows participants to see the tree take shape in real time, making the integration of prior sessions' content visually and experientially concrete. The progressive reveal of the tree as each section is completed embodies the learning arc itself.

config.concept: Identifying and engaging a Core Values mentor: criteria and candidate selection
config.description: A five-criteria scoring widget where participants enter up to three mentor candidate names and rate each on: (1) mature, committed Christian who will give honest feedback, (2) demonstrates Core Values in personal and professional life, (3) has successfully executed a personal or career breakthrough, (4) proven leadership qualities, (5) willingness to attend a two-hour orientation and meet monthly. A 1–4 scale (not a strength / average / above average / significant strength) populates a comparison bar chart for all three candidates. The widget flags any candidate who scores below 2 on the breakthrough criterion — the most important quality — with a red indicator and a prompt to reconsider. Participants can add notes to explain each rating.
config.rationale: The comparative bar chart forces participants to make genuine distinctions between candidates rather than selecting the most convenient person — a key DVULI insight is that mentor selection is often compromised by relational comfort rather than fit, and the visual comparison makes this pattern visible.

config.concept: Reflective journaling: the two-column reflective loop method
config.description: A two-column journaling interface that mirrors the DVULI format. The right column is labeled 'Response to Events' and prompts participants to write their immediate reflections on their DISC and LSA results. The left column is labeled 'Reflective Loop — Deeper Insights' and is locked for 24 hours after the right-column entry is saved (simulating the delayed reflection discipline). When unlocked, the left column populates with three spiraling questions: 'What deeper pattern do you notice?' 'How has your thinking shifted since you first wrote this?' and 'What action does this insight demand?' A timeline view shows prior entries and their reflective loops, modeling the ongoing learning history discipline.
config.rationale: The time-locked left column embeds the spacing principle directly into the tool's design — participants cannot short-circuit the reflective loop by immediately answering both columns, because the 24-hour delay is built into the interface, creating the distributed practice that makes journaling a genuine learning discipline rather than a performance.
